The key word here, of course, is affordable. Affordable to one doesn't necessarily mean it's affordable to all. So, how did Kipingers decide that these eight places were affordable? Ultimately, Kipingers based its findings on the cost of living index, which is developed by the Council for Community and Economic Research. But it didn't just stop there. Its list was also based on warm weather and also plentiful housing options and also access to lots of activities that help keep you feeling young and access to good health care just in case you hit a rough patch while you're away. - The Untold Truth of Snowbird Brown… What REALLY Happened After The Show?: Kind: captions Language: en You thought you knew Snowbird Brown, but what if I told you she almost lost everything? Her family, her health, and the life she once knew. This is the story no one is talking about. You probably remember her as the quiet, wild-hearted girl from the wilderness. But what really happened to Snowbird Brown after the cameras stopped rolling? Because behind that calm personality, there's a story filled with pain, survival, and a life that completely changed. Snowbird Brown, also known as Birdie, became famous through the hit reality show Alaskan Bush People. A show that followed the Brown family living deep in the wilderness, far away from modern society. But here's what many people don't realize. That life wasn't as simple as it looked. From a very young age, Snowbird was different from the rest of her family. While others were loud and adventurous, she was quiet, deeply connected to nature, and incredibly emotional. She didn't need attention. She didn't chase fame. She just wanted peace. And maybe that's why fans connected with her the most. But everything changed. In 2021, tragedy hit the Brown family hard. Their father, Billy Brown, the man who built their entire lifestyle, suddenly passed away after a seizure. And Snowbird was there. She witnessed those final moments and that moment broke something inside her. Losing not just a father but the leader of the family. After his death, everything started falling apart. The family dynamic changed. Responsibilities shifted and Snowbird had to grow stronger whether she was ready or not. But then something even more terrifying happened. In 2022, Snowbird began experiencing severe abdominal pain. At first, she ignored it just like someone used to surviving harsh conditions would. But the pain got worse. So bad that she couldn't even stand properly. Eventually, she was rushed to the hospital. And that's when doctors discovered something shocking. A large tumor inside her body. At that moment, everything changed again. There was fear, uncertainty, and the possibility that her life could take a completely different turn. She had to undergo surgery. And while the operation was successful, the emotional impact stayed with her. Imagine going from living freely in the wild to suddenly fighting for your own health. That kind of shift changes a person. But here's where Snowbird surprised everyone. Instead of breaking down, she started rebuilding herself. After the show slowed down and eventually stopped, Snowbird chose a different path. She didn't chase Hollywood. She didn't try to become a typical celebrity. Instead, she went back to what she loved the most, nature, animals, and a simple life. She even started creating handmade items like dreamcatchers and crafts, turning her passion into something meaningful. And slowly she began building her own identity. Not as just a member of the Brown family, but as Snowbird, her own person. At the same time, the Brown family itself was facing major struggles. After Billy's death, tensions started rising. There were rumors, conflicts, and even legal issues involving some family members. The once tight-knit family was no longer the same. And through all of this, Snowbird stayed mostly quiet. No drama, no public fights, just distance. And maybe that silence says everything. Because sometimes the strongest people are the ones who choose peace over chaos. Today, Snowbird continues to live a low-key life. She spends time with animals, stays connected to nature, and occasionally shares glimpses of her life online. But one question still remains. Is she truly happy? Or is she still carrying the weight of everything she went through? Because when you look deeper, her story isn't just about survival in the wilderness. It's about surviving loss, fear, and change. And maybe that's why people are still so drawn to her. Not because she's loud, not because she's dramatic, but because she's real. And in a world full of noise, that's rare. So the next time you see Snowbird Brown, remember you're not just looking at a reality TV star. You're looking at someone who faced loss, fear, and uncertainty and still chose to keep going.

- WHAT IS THE RIGHT SNOWBOARD STANCE FOR YOU?: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Your stance on a snowboard depends on many factors, your height, weight, ability and riding style are just a few, but when we talk about stance we mean two things: your stance width and binding angles. The ‘stance width’ is the distance between the centre of your bindings when mounted onto a snowboard. After years of development and consulting with scientists and doctors, we recommend finding your ‘natural stance’ by measuring the distance from the floor to the centre of your knee. When a rider stands with their feet this distance apart, it allows for the optimum balance between stability and comfort, providing an active and reactive body position that also reduces stress on your joints. When attaching bindings to a snowboard, you can rotate the mounting discs to point them in different directions, these are called your ‘binding angles’. The angle is defined as zero when aligned perpendicular to the length of the board, negative when angled towards the tail and positive when angled towards the nose. Angles are always shown on binding discs in three-degree increments to help you dial in your stance when mounting your bindings. For an all-mountain or beginner rider, we recommend a stance width of your natural stance plus two centimetres and binding angles of between plus fifteen and twenty-four degrees in the front, and between minus six degrees and plus six in the rear. This is the optimum mix of performance and comfort, while giving a great base for sidehits, park and powder. For a freestyle or park-focused set up, stance plus six centimetres and binding angles of between plus six and twenty-one degrees in the front, and between zero and minus fifteen degrees in the rear. This gives the best balance and stability when landing jumps or riding switch. For a freeride-focused set up, stance plus four centimetres and binding angles of between plus twenty-one and thirty degrees in the front, plus nine degrees in the rear. This gives great stability in rough terrain or at high speeds and makes turning or landing in powder super easy. For a carving set up, we recommend a stance width of your natural stance and binding angles of between plus twenty-four and thirty-six degrees in the front, plus fifteen degrees in the rear. This is perfect for holding an edge and driving through turns. Every board has a ‘reference stance’ marked on the topsheet to provide a starting point This is the optimum mounting point developed for each board. Make sure you adjust your stance width starting from this reference, moving the bindings outwards or inwards in equal proportions so that your centre of gravity always remains above the same point on the board. Of course, this is just a starting point. By experimenting, over time you’ll find the perfect combination of stance width, setback and angles for your body type, riding style and ability. Let us know what your snowboard stance is in the comments below, and don’t forget to check out our other guides for choosing and setting up your snowboard.

- Zero Trust Explained in 4 mins: Kind: captions Language: en-US Zero Trust is a Security Strategy that says&nbsp; you shouldn’t grant implicit trust to a user, device or an application, based solely&nbsp; upon some property about them, like their network location. Over the next&nbsp; few minutes I’ll explain exactly what we mean by this, and how IBM can help.&nbsp; Let’s be clear up front - Zero Trust isn’t something that can simply be delivered by&nbsp; implementing a new piece of technology, nor is it a point product or service&nbsp; that you can just go out and buy. It’s a Security strategy that has three core&nbsp; principles. But before I come onto those, let me explain why organisations are increasingly&nbsp; moving on from the previous popular model of Perimeter Security: Firstly&nbsp; - there’s this somewhat Medieval notion that you have a perimeter to your network,&nbsp; where you build the walls as high as possible and try stop malicious actors at the gates.&nbsp; This no longer works because employees are working from home more than&nbsp; they're working from the office,&nbsp;&nbsp; and because hybrid cloud is&nbsp; now clearly the pre-eminent platform for enterprise infrastructure.&nbsp; So it’s an increasingly complex problem to define a perimeter. Secondly -&nbsp; the concept of Trust is a very human one that we’ve taught computers to&nbsp; adapt to - for example, if I see Helen every day at the office wearing her employee badge,&nbsp; I trust that she’s an employee and is there for the right reasons. In reality, I don’t&nbsp; actually know that she wasn’t let go last week for misconduct, and is now back in&nbsp; the office trying to steal corporate data. So a computer security model based on a human&nbsp; definition of trust is inherently flawed, particularly in a world where attackers are&nbsp; finding it easier than ever to steal credentials and disguise themselves as trustworthy.&nbsp; Without a Zero Trust Security model, once an attacker is in the corporate network, they&nbsp; can move laterally to new systems with relative ease. This brings me onto&nbsp; the first defining principle of the Zero Trust model - “Never trust,&nbsp; always Verify” Just because somebody is on your corporate network, and is carrying&nbsp; that badge with an employee name on it, doesn’t mean they are who they say they are, or&nbsp; that they’re necessarily well-intentioned. So Always Verify piece refers to the fact that&nbsp;&nbsp; every time something, like a&nbsp; user, device or application tries to make a new connection attempt,&nbsp;&nbsp; that attempt should be rigorously&nbsp; authenticated and authorised, and not simply trusted because it’s coming&nbsp; from inside the corporate network, for example. Implement least privilege is the second core&nbsp; principle of a Zero Trust Architecture which says you should only grant users and applications&nbsp; the minimum amount of access that they need to perform their job effectively, and no more.&nbsp; Privileged Access Management is a great way of implementing least privilege&nbsp; for Admin users, for example. And finally, Assume Breach. This is my favourite&nbsp; of the Zero Trust principles because it encourages teams to plan for the worst case scenario,&nbsp; and build robust and tested incident response plans so that when attacks do occur, the&nbsp; time to respond is rapid & well-practiced. Not only this, but this principle encourages&nbsp; organisations to shrink the target, and the impact zone of an attack, through networking&nbsp; principles like micro-segmentation. So, how can IBM Security help? We&nbsp; recognise that different clients will have different business drivers & priorities for&nbsp; why they want to deploy Zero Trust, so we’ve created four actionable blueprints, depending&nbsp; on where you want to start - they are: • Reduce the risk of insider threat • Secure the remote workforce • Preserve customer privacy • Protect the hybrid cloud You can download these, with&nbsp; no form filling required, from www.ibm.com Thanks for watching

  Top Comments:

2. Provide least privilege necessary to get job done
3. Assume breach at every step
  2. @tariqkhan185 [2 likes]: I knew there was something off about Helen.
  3. @MariusBarczak [1 likes]: Zero Trust is often misunderstood as a product strategy or a collection of security tools. In reality, it is an architectural philosophy that fundamentally changes how organizations think about trust, access and system design.

For many years companies tried to solve security problems by building stronger perimeters. Firewalls became more advanced, detection systems more complex and monitoring more extensive. But the real problem was never just technology. The real problem was architectural thinking.

Modern environments are no longer static. Cloud computing, remote work, AI systems and distributed infrastructure mean that the traditional perimeter no longer exists. Identity, context and behavior now define trust, not network location.

This is why security architecture is becoming the most critical discipline in cybersecurity. Organizations that succeed will be those that design security into their systems from the beginning instead of trying to add protection later.

Zero Trust is not about trusting nothing. It is about validating everything intelligently.

It is about designing systems where compromise of one component does not mean compromise of the whole environment. This requires segmentation, strong identity models, continuous verification and resilience thinking.

In the next years I believe we will see a major shift from tool-driven security strategies to architecture-driven security strategies. The organizations that understand this early will gain a massive advantage in resilience, stability and long-term risk reduction.

Cybersecurity maturity will increasingly be measured not by how many tools a company has, but by how well their security architecture is designed.

- What Is Snowbird Tourism? - Everything About Phoenix: Kind: captions Language: en What is snowbird tourism? Have you ever wondered why so many people flock to Phoenix during the winter months? This phenomenon is known as snowbird tourism. It involves seasonal migration, where individuals, often from colder northern parts of North America, travel to warmer southern areas to escape the harsh winter. Phoenix, Arizona is a prime destination for these travelers seeking sunshine and milder temperatures. Each year, thousands of snowbirds make their way to Phoenix, significantly impacting the local economy. These visitors typically stay for several months, which can lead to a noticeable increase in the city's population during winter. This influx supports local businesses from restaurants to shops and recreational facilities, providing a boost to the economy. One of the appealing aspects of snowbird tourism in Phoenix is the wide range of activities available. Snowbirds can enjoy outdoor adventures like hiking and biking, cultural events, and worldclass golf courses. The city offers various accommodations including vacation home rentals and recreational vehicle parks catering to different preferences and budgets. However, the large number of snowbirds does come with challenges. The increased population can strain local resources such as transportation and housing. Additionally, the rise in vehicle usage during the winter months can lead to air pollution, which is a concern for both residents and visitors alike. Despite these challenges, Phoenix continues to embrace snowbirds as part of its vibrant winter community. The city benefits from the economic boost and the cultural exchange that comes with hosting these seasonal visitors. Local officials and community leaders work to balance the needs of both residents and snowbirds, ensuring that Phoenix remains an attractive destination for those seeking a warm winter retreat. In summary, snowbird tourism plays a vital role in Phoenix's history and cultural heritage travel. It reflects the city's growth and the rise of tourism, showcasing how seasonal visitors contribute to the local economy while enjoying all that Phoenix has to offer.

Many people have lost their understandings of the role of the matriarch in Cherokee communities, but TJ truly exemplified what it is to be a Cherokee man, by holding our women to the highest regard and honoring their foundational role in the Snowbird community. Since this was one of TJ's last projects, let's hear a little bit from him on the basis of why some of these women were selected. - [TJ] In the day, in the traditional sense, each town had their beloved people that really went above and beyond to ensure that those things that make us Anigiduwagi, that make us Cherokee, still continue, and provide that sense of identity for future generations. And we're hoping that this mural can just be an outward showing of how the community reveres these individuals and to honor their contributions to our community. Thank you for taking the time to listen to this, and look forward to someday meeting you all, as our paths have come beside one another. Take care. - Learn Real American English: The American Snowbird! | Comprehensible Input B1 Intermediate: Kind: captions Language: en Hello everybody. My name is teacher Brian with English sponge. At English sponge, we believe that listening is learning. So if you clicked on this video to listen to some English and to learn how to speak English, then you clicked on the right video. Be sure to go to our web page, englishsponge.com, for hours and hours of English language content that you can listen to and learn from. In this video, you're going to listen to me, teacher Brian, talk about a specific type of American human. That American human is called the snowbird. >> [music] >> The American snowbird is a person who lives in the northern states. I'm talking about the upper mid-Atlantic like the states of Delaware, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, or New York where I live. The American snowbird can also live in New England like the states of Massachusetts or New Hampshire, Maine, or Vermont. And the American snowbird has also been known to live in the upper Midwest like in Minnesota or Illinois or Wisconsin. Now the thing that the American snowbird of all these regions shares in common is that their habitat in the winter months turns frigid. It gets very cold particularly of course the more north that you travel. So as you all know the cold weather can become quite uncomfortable and for the American snowbird this frigid weather becomes intolerable. So the American snowbird like their brothers of the sky migrate south in the winter. Specifically, the American snowbird goes to a state called Florida. Florida is the southernmost state on the eastern seabboard. And in Florida, it is almost always warm, which is exactly the type of climate that the American snowbird desires. So, right now I'm going to the grocery store so I can buy some snacks from my train trip down south to Florida because I am an American snowbird. As you can see, I have successfully managed my southernly migration south. I left glorious New York City and I have arrived in the sunshine of Florida. Truly wonderful down here. Just look around. Clear, bright, blue skies, a blazing sun, plenty of warmth. I'm going to turn the camera around now to let you see what a typical neighborhood looks like in Florida. You can see the green grass. You can see the palm trees. You can even see the deciduous trees still have their leaves on them because it's not cold enough for them to fall off. Now, where I just came from New York City, rather after I left New York City, one day after I left, the skies dumped over 5 in of snow upon the ground. five inches. In addition to that, that blizzard was followed up by days of near freezing cold rain. So, for the snowbird, that can be intolerable. It's not too comfortable. However, where I'm walking today in Florida, it's bright, it's sunny, and it's 75°. It's quite wonderful. Now, in order to adapt to this new climate, you can see that I've shed my winter clothes. When I was in New York, I was wearing a thick hooded Northace winter jacket that's gone and it's replaced by a short sleeved shirt. Short sleeves. I also don't have my long johns on anymore because there's no need to keep warm because the air is warm. I've also got rid of my hoodie. There's no need for that because again, it's warm. It's no longer cold. This is what the American snowbird is looking for when he heads south. So, what we'll do here is we'll turn around and we'll start talking a little bit about the things that the American snowbird likes to do when he arrives in the southern clims. What does the American snowbird like to do for recreation? Well, first of all, walking. Look at this. Beautiful. There's no ice to contend with. And let me tell you, the winters in New York can be quite treacherous because what occurs after a good thorough rain or a snow is that there's the development of black ice. Black ice is deceptive and dangerous. The ice assumes the same color as the pavement of the streets. And so you'll be walking, doing just fine, going to the grocery store or wherever, and you'll slip and you'll fall. This has happened to me on multiple occasions. In fact, probably about once a year, I'll end up falling, slipping on the black ice. And now, the problem with this is that as I age, it's getting a bit more difficult to recover from. The older viewers will certainly sympathize with that. But here in Florida, there's no black ice because it doesn't get that cold. Or I should say it rarely gets that cold. It's typically pretty warm even in central Florida where I am right now. I'm not even in South Florida and it's still very, very nice in terms of the weather. So, what else do this American snowbirds enjoy doing for recreation when they head south? Well, like I said, they enjoy walking. They also enjoy playing different types of sports. I'm going to get some footage later of swimming. The American snowbird loves to swim. The American snowbird also enjoys playing pickle ball. I'll get some footage of that. And generally any outdoor sports that they would normally do in the summer up north, they can enjoy in the winter down south, which is the entire point, isn't it? Now, one sport that they enjoy playing is golf. The American snowbird loves playing golf. Golf, if you don't know, is a sport where the players will, for no good reason, interrupt a perfectly good walk by trying to hit a small white ball into a very small hole. And they do this 18 times. Why? I'm not sure. I don't like golf, but a lot of people do. So, I'm going to cross this street and we are going to watch some people play golf. Here we are. We are on a golf course. And you could see these people. Let me zoom in playing golf here. They are on the green. Now, these people are wearing long sleeve shirts even though it's 75°. So, what that tells me is that these people here are not American snowbirds. These people are probably natives to this region because it's only 75° and yet these people have on long sleeves. I've noticed this. You can tell when you're in the south who is a snowbird and who is a native. The snowbirds when it gets down to 75 70° he'll be dressed in a short sleeve shirt like I am. But the natives who are accustomed to 90° weather, 95 degree weather, these people will be dressed as if they're ready to summit Everest. You know, they get very cold very quickly. So they'll be all bundled up despite the fact that it's only 70 75 degrees. And with this walk now complete, I'd like to show you some footage of some of the recreation that the American snowbird will engage in. So enjoy. One of the most beloved forms of recreation for the American snowbird is swimming. In Florida, they have plenty of swimming pools. Now, the American snowbird will use the swimming pool not only for fitness, to engage his body, grow his muscles and his lung capacity. He will also use the pool for recreation, to have fun, and most importantly, to cool off on these grueling hot winter days. Now, as it happens today, we have with us a young American snowbird who is enjoying his recreational time in the pool. >> And we can see this young man right over here. So, let's go get a better look. This is a young American snowbird. As you can see, he's ready for his swim in the pool. He has his swimming trunks, his bathing suit. He has his goggles to protect his eyes from the chemical chlorine that is used in the swimming pool. And he's prepared his body with sunblock so the vicious South Florida sun doesn't damage his skin. And so now the young American snowbird will jump into the pool to show how he enjoys swimming. >> Fascinating. Fascinating. Another popular recreational sport for the American snowbird is the game of pickle ball. Pickle ball has gained more notoriety in the United States of America over the course of the past 10 years. It is a very fun game. If you haven't played, migrate down south like I did and try it out for yourself. The game of pickle ball. Hello students of English sponge. As you can see, I have returned to my beloved New York City. You'll note my surroundings. You'll also note my winter clothing. As it turns out, I am not a true As we learned in this video, the American snowbird is a human who lives in the northern states and then migrates south for the warm weather of typically Florida. At this stage of my life, I can't spend the entirety of the winter months in Florida. I have to come back to New York for family and professional reasons. So, I am not a true American snowbird. Not yet. Anyway, with that said, I hope that you have found this video interesting and I hope you've learned a little bit about American culture. They're still here. So, wherever you first time, find a comfortable place, take a deep breath, relax, and settle in because this is one of those stories. They're called snowbirds. This is a term applied to someone from the north who winters in more temperate climates in the south in order to escape the cold and snow and ice. Much of the modern-day population of Dolphin Island are seasonal transients. Certainly, there are many folks who visit the island during the tourist seasons, such as for spring bird watching and banding season and spring breaks, which encompasses about 8 weeks for the annual fishing rodeo and for several other events throughout the year. But there are also a significant number of folks from northern areas who spend the winter months, the winter season on the island and around the area. And they do this in RVs and multiple campgrounds and RV parks on the island and near in multiple condos on the island. A number of them own homes on the island that they spend several months out of the year in. But these seasonal transients aren't the first such in the history of the island. The oldest man-made, we'll call them structures. The oldest man-made structures on the island speak to an earlier group of transients, a prehistoric transient people who archaeologists believe made a regular pilgrimage to winter on the island, much like the snowbirds do today. These were the Bottle Creek people, those whom we might call the original snowbirds. In this case, I'm applying the term in a tongue and cheek way to be sure to people from the Mississippian era. These indigenous people who left the Bottle Creek area near modern-day Stockton, Alabama, not the north and and no, they didn't have snow typically, but they're still several miles north of what is today modern-day Dolphin Island. And they came down to winter on the island. Now, Bottle Creek is an indigenous mounds site near, as we said earlier, Stockton, Alabama in the Mobile, Tensaw Delta. It's about 20 mi above modern-day Mobile. And this site is, as noted, from the Mississippian era or the Mississippian period. This site is approximately 50 to 55 miles north, as the crow flies from the shell mounds on Dolphin Island. And at the Bottle Creek area, there are 18 or so mounds located there. Archaeologists believe that it was occupied from about 1250 AD onward well into the 18th century and it was an important site for indigenous people such as the Mobileians from whom Mobile, Alabama takes its name. It's believed to have served as the principal political and religious center for the region, which extended all the way down to the Gulf in to modern-day Pensacola into that area and across into at least modern-day southern Mississippi. Of all the mound sites in Alabama, Bottle Creek is considered to be second in importance only to Moundville near Tuscaloosa. These are the people who archaeologists believe began building, and that's in my air quotes, the Shell mounds. And I use air quotes there because even though building is what they did, it was actually more piling up their trash than building houses or other types of what we would normally consider structures or buildings. The mounds that they built are called midens, which means they're trash heaps. These are where the people piled the refues from harvesting oysters and fish. And for the record, archaeologists love trash piles of ancient peoples because that's where you get to see how the people of that time were living. You see in what they throw away, what tools they used, what they were eating, how they were adapting. And conversely, what's not there tells you what they weren't using or eating or so forth. Archaeologists have theorized that the Bottle Creek people were going to the north shore of Dolphin Island because it may have provided a kind of security. There was plenty of food and it and it seems that there were lots of oysters and fish available in what is today known as Little Dolphin Island Sound or also known as Dolphin Island Bay. At that time, scientists believe that the water level would have been at least a foot lower, which would have made the bay shallower and would have made it easier to harvest the oysters. So, this was a place where they generally would have had plenty to eat. This was a place to smoke those fish and oysters to preserve them for future use. This was a place to spend the winter in relatively temperate climates. If there were issues with famine, with drought, with floods, some other reason for times of food shortage in their home area around Bottle Creek, then Dolphin Island seems that it would have been a place to fall back on to ensure that their food stocks were maintained. And because these people used this location year after year after year after year, century after century after century, these mittens grew substantially over time. When the archaeologists have done research in the mittens, they found that they typically have multiple layers, layers of shells, layers of broken pottery called potshards and other refues and layers of charcoal where they had fires. These mittens were originally in something of a serpentine ring, similar to rings found throughout the southeast on the coast, particularly in South Carolina and Georgia. Part of the ring in the midens on Dolphin Island have been destroyed, though. More on that later. This also appears to have been an area where trade with other parts of the Gulf and the southeast went on because there are a number of non-native plants in the area of the Shell Mounds. One type of plant that does appear there that is native to the area, there are a number of magnificent old live oaks on the Shell mounds and in the area. These are massive and estimates are that they're close to 200 to 300 years old. I've seen at least one source stating that some of these trees are at least 800 years old and that they were already mature trees when the first Europeans arrived in the area. This is highly unlikely. As a general rule, live oaks tend to live 200 to 300 years on average, and some will occasionally reach 400 to 500 years old, but 800 years old for a live oak is pretty much unheard of. One story about the live oaks that's told is that there evidently was one of those trees in this area that was the site for marriages for some of the indigenous people. These were called giveaways or handfasting. Often these would occur around a sacred fire and these rituals honor the sacred witness of the tree in the ceremony. It's possible, of course, but unlikely that this tree is still in existence in the Shellmound area if this practice was observed in the area more than a couple hundred years ago. Nevertheless, that there are old live oaks there in that area means that if none of them are that actual live oak tree used in these ceremonies, then the trees that are there are likely the descendants of that old oak. And then at some point, and I don't know that anyone is sure exactly when this started, the Mississippian people who were creating these mittens began to use at least some of them as burial sites. These particular midens have not been extensively examined for burials. However, archaeologists did discover at least a small number of remains, human remains in a portion of the mounds and some limited excavations that were done there. And based on at least one situation that happened during the island's history, it's likely that some of the mounds were fairly extensively used for this practice. Again, more on that shortly. At some point, the peoples of the Mississippian period disappeared. Whether they died out or moved away or were assimilated later into some other historic period tribes, that's unclear. Nevertheless, the tribes of the historic periods, that is, those who were on the island when the first European settlers arrived, began using the island in much the same way as those earlier peoples had done, as a place to gather food. It also was most definitely a calat site as well. Now, the word calat comes from the French word caling read or pipe. It's a term used to describe a site where the peace pipe would be shared. It's come to be a broader term used to apply generically to any place where intertribal peace was observed regardless of whether a pipe was actually involved or not. for the tribes of the historic period such as the Chakaw and the Muscogeogis who are commonly known in the area as the Creeks. This seems to have been a a a site for different peoples to meet on peaceful terms. And in the case of the Shell Mounds, Calumet was not a generic term. It's actually a documented site where indigenous tribes and French explorers smoked to seal alliances, most notably in 1717. These midens may have been among the highest points on the island when they were at their peak usage. And and this speaks to just how much they've been used over the centuries because some of the midens even now are several feet tall, 10 to 12 feet, perhaps even more in some cases. So this also means that during flooding or storm surge, the midens might actually have been among the safest places to be. That said, it likely was storms eroding some of the midens where burials had occurred that resulted in the island's first name given to it by Europeans, Isle Massacre or Massacre Island. At the end of January 1699, French explorer Pierre Lemony Sier Draville came into the area and as he and his men passed by what is now Babatri and Cedar Point, they encountered what we now call Dolphin Island. As they arrived, they discovered a large pile of bones, possibly from as many as 60 men and women. Presuming these remains to be the result of a slaughter, Iberville called the island is massacre. As noted already, just a few years later, the mounds were then a place for the French explorers to meet with indigenous tribes in the area in 1717 to negotiate with them. And it was definitely at this point, if not before, that the site became a Calumet site through that meeting and through the agreements that were sealed through the smoking of the pipe. How much the mounds continued to be used throughout the colonial period though isn't well documented that I was able to find. In 1821, as noted in the previous episode, construction began on Fort Gaines, and that continued in fits and starts throughout the next three decades. At least one account that I've come across indicates that the area of the mound ring that's now missing is the result of mining the mounds for material for making mortar for the construction of the fort. Just as with the materials extracted from Greek and Roman ruins by later Europeans, structures that were created by indigenous peoples, whether as mere trash heaps or as intentional structures, have been seen as viable sources of building materials throughout the history of the development of the United States. And it would appear that the shell mounds on Dolphin Island are no different. The term for this practice is spolia and it's been practiced by peoples probably since long before records were ever kept. So if that's how part of the mounds were destroyed or even if it were for some other project. It's I'll I'll say this very clearly. This is sad. This is tragic in many ways. But it's by no means an isolated incident. Throughout the rest of the 1800s and early 1900s, the small number of residents of the island ran livestock such as goats and cattle. These animals kept much of the vegetation that now extensively covers the mounds grazed down such that the mounds were much more clearly visible than they are now. Even in the 1960s and 70s, the mounds were clearly visible for residents of the island. At some point, the state of Alabama assumed ownership of the site and it was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1973. Today, most of the Shell Mounds are part of the Indian Shell Mound Park, which is administered by the Alabama Department of Conservation and Natural Resources. a small portion of them are on the property of the US Food and Drug Administration facility that is adjacent to the park. And so that brings us up to the present and and I'll say this this I find this interesting and that is in spite of the large numbers of bird watchers and nature lovers who visit the area and there are quite a few of those every year but in spite of them the Indian Shellmound Park is perhaps one of the island's bestkept secrets. I've talked to quite a few people who have been to the island several times and have never visited it even once. A and the same is even true for many of the locals. I had a brief conversation with Dolphin Island Mayor Jeff Collier who grew up on the island and he said that when he was growing up his parents instructed him not to go there presumably due to there being burials and at least part of them. But in the years since livestock was on the island and grazing and keeping everything, we'll say, mowed down, the shell mounds have become an unruly and beautiful mass of vegetation that's a haven for birds of all types. And it's also a haven for the bird watchers who delight in observing them, photographing them, and documenting them. And this is partly because Dolphin Island is uniquely located for what are called fallout events. These events happen mostly in the springtime of the year when northward flying migratory birds encounter bad weather such as storms or stiff headwinds. These types of events lead the migrating birds to fall out to the nearest available landmass for rest and refeeding. For birds migrating across the Gulf of Mexico, or if you prefer, the Gulf of America, the first such land mass for many of these birds is Dolphin Island. And so these exhausted birds stop on Dolphin Island to rest, to recuperate, to eat, and to build their strength back up so that they can continue their journey. And so as such, Dolphin Island, the entire island, is a bird sanctuary in one sense of the word, but there are several designated natural areas that are preserved around the island to serve as sanctuaries for these migratory birds. And the Indian Shell Mounds Park is one such location. Not only does the park have, as we've already described, lots of trees and vegetation for these birds to take sanctuary in, it also has hiking trails crisscrossing the mounds. There are these large live oak trees there, as well as other types of trees. There are also numerous memorial park benches that are scattered throughout the park that are available for people who are bird watching and like to sit and and just meditate and enjoy the natural surroundings there. But during these fallout events, the Indian Shell Mounds Park is a haven for many such migratory birds there. It's also a very peaceful place. Even when there are several folks at the site, people tend to speak in hushed tones and to honor the presence of nature. And of course, as we've already mentioned, there are sure to be at least a few bird watchers there nearly anytime you visit. Interestingly, there also is now a pair of bald eagles in the area. There's a communication tower that's on the shore between the conservation and natural resources facility and the FDA facility. The bald eagles like to perch a top that tower. They've also engaged in nesting on that tower in times past as well. And unfortunately, those nests can't be permitted to remain. Bald eagle nests are built over time and they're built up over time and as a result they can become massive and as a result they can become very very heavy. These are generally constructed of very large limbs and sticks and even small logs and they're built in layers. As each brood grows to maturity, to be frank, the nest fills with excrement and debris, and so new layers are simply built on top of the old, and thus the weight of the nest becomes immense over time. If allowed to remain, this would damage the communication tower in time. So whenever the birds try to create a nest on that tower, this has to be removed. >> [music] >> If you'd like to support what we're doing here, we do have merchandise available through the Summers Media Enterprises website. In the 1950s and60s, the intra coastal waterway was created and as part of the creation of this commercial shipping system, canals were dug where there was no previous waterway and channels were dredged to make deeper waterways where waterways already existed. These changes certainly facilitated and enhanced commercial maritime traffic, but they also had the unintended consequences of changing estuarine ecology of Mobile Bay, Bons Secur Bay, the Mississippi Sound, Dolphin Island Bay, and elsewhere. So the conditions that for centuries had been so perfect for the Bottle Creek people to harvest all the oysters and fish that they needed to sustain them no longer exist as they did then. In some parts of the region, the oysters are returning. Babacry on the other side of the Mississippi sound is seeing a resurgence in oyster production, such as with oyster farms like Murder Point Oysters and others. but they're not naturally occurring in Dolphin Island Bay in the numbers remotely resembling previous centuries. I say all that to say this. Those conditions that led the Bottle Creek people to winter on what's now Dolphin Island and by and through which they created the shell mounds there, those no longer exist. This story draws from a combination of archaeological research, historical writing, and modern interpretation of the Dolphin Island Shell Mounds. I leaned heavily on academic work related to shell mound archaeology in the southeastern United States, including Rebecca Saunders chapter, Archaic Shell Mounds in the American Southeast, from the Oxford Handbook of Topics in Archaeology. That work provides important context for how archaeologists understand shell mounds, not just as refuge piles, but as meaningful cultural landscapes tied to ritual, community, and long-term use. For regional context, I use material from the Encyclopedia of Alabama entry on the Bottle Creek site, which helped establish the importance of that site as a political and cultural center in the Mobile Tensaw Delta. I also referenced interpretive material from the University of Alabama's Office of Archaeological Research along with additional historical summaries and archived articles related to the Dolphin Island shell mounds and their use over time. Some of the interpretive framing in this episode, particularly the idea of seasonal movement to the island and the role of shellfish as a reliable food source, draws from archaeological interpretations attributed to Dr. Gregory Waselov and others working in this region. For more local historical perspective, I consulted A History of Dolphin Island Under Five Flags by Francis Young, which provides insight into how the island has been used and understood across different periods. I also made use of a variety of coastal and environmental resources related to Dolphin Island, including material on the island's role as a migratory bird habitat and its designation as part of Alabama's coastal conservation system. >> [sighs] >> Finally, I'm very appreciative of John Moresca of the Alabama Marine Resources Division of the Conservation and Natural Resources Department who took the time to talk at length with me about the Indian Shell Mound Park, including giving me a guided tour around the area. - More Than Just A Snowbird: the story of the Canadair CT-114 Tutor: Kind: captions Language: en-CA The snowbirds are the most visible component of the Royal Canadian Air Force. Painted in their iconic red and white pattern, they tour North America putting on stunning aerial displays. The workhorse behind the snowbirds for over 30 years is the humble the Canadair CT-114 Tutor, a high performance jet trainer with a long history with the RCAF. The Tutor was the first in house design for Canadair. Based in Montreal, Canadair had until then made a name for themselves building and upgrading foreign designs. These included the CL-30 Shooting Star trainer, the CL-13 Sabre fighter, and the C-4 North Star transport. In the early 1950s Canada was operating jet aircraft like the Sabre and the Canuck but new aircraft were being developed at an enormous rate. Higher performance aircraft with expanded flight profiles like the upcoming American Starfighter demonstrated the need for an expanded training program for pilots. Canadair initiated a two seat jet trainer program with the intention of replacing the existing propeller driven fleet and to keep pace with advancements. It was hoped that the design would be completed ahead of the anticipated contracts from the RCAF and other allied air forces for a new jet powered lead-in trainer. There were other designs being developed concurrently by countries including the United States and the United Kingdom. Aircraft like the popular Cessna T-37 Tweet and the BAC Jet Provost had their first flights in 1954, around the same time the Tutor was being designed. Despite this late start Canadair was confident that they could compete in the allied market for jet trainers. In February of 1951, designer karl Irbitis, who would later go one to design the innovative CL-84 vertical take off and landing swing-wing aircraft, proposed the first version was simply called the Jet Trainer. It was to be produced in several versions to reflect the operational requirements. In the primary trainer role the Tutor was supposed to have a wide flight envelope in order to replace both the Harvard and CT-133 Silver Star trainers already being used. The initial design series was oriented towards perfecting the spin initiation and recovery performance to align it with the training The CL-41-1 was proposed in January of 1954. It had most of the features present in the final production design but had a low set tail wing. Wind tunnel tests found this configuration to be too sensitive to student inputs when entering and exiting a spin and so the design was revised. The CL-41-2 had a T-tail and went through many iterations between 1954 and 1956 to determine its optimal position along the tail rudder. Using models in a wind tunnel, the wing was raised incrementally from its low set position to the familiar T-tail configuration to determine the best placement. In Jan of 1956, the CL-41-3 was proposed. It had a tandem seat configuration similar to that of the Harvard and CT-133. It was not pursued beyond paper studies due to the fact that the RCAF had indicated that they wanted a side by side trainer. The CL-41-4 was a further modified version of the CL-41-2 model. It included a larger tail rudder, increased cord for the vertical fin and refinements surrounding the placement of the tail wing. This model was tested extensively and showed the desired flight characteristics. By mid-1956 wooden mockups were built and the design was more or less finalised and Canadair began soliciting governments to fund a flying prototype. Funding for the construction of two prototypes was secured in Sept 1956. The government of Canada decided to jointly fund the project alongside Canadair and the project began to pick up momentum. The CL-41A, was designed to be well suited to the lead-in trainer role. It first flew on January 13th 1960. Performance of the new jet was respectable and compared favorably to its contemporaries. The tutor consisted of two main all-metal semi-monocoque structural units connected by four bolts. The forward section included the nose, cockpit and center fuselage, while the rear section included the engine and tail surfaces. The wings were mated to the center fuselage. A pair of speed brakes were located halfway down the tail section. This configuration enabled easy engine replacement and maintenance. 45 panels around the aircraft provided ground crews with easy access for any maintenance needs. The first prototype was equipped with a Pratt & Whitney built JT12A-1 engine. Later this would be replaced by the Orenda-built General Electric J85-CAN-49 two stage turbine producing 1,180kg of thrust. This propelled the 3,357kg aircraft to a top speed of 888 km/h up to a maximum altitude of 13,100m. The Tutor had a wingspan of 11.13m, a length of 9.75m, and stood 2.82m at the tail. The side-by-side cockpit was large and spacious with a wraparound rear hinged canopy. It seated the student on the left and the instructor on the right. This configuration allowed students to directly observe the examples given by the instructor as well as allowing the instructor to directly evaluate the progress of the student. The crew sat on a pair of Weber ROCAT ejection seats which allowed a zero-altitude, zero-speed ejection. The tutor featured a wide wheel base for good directional stability on the ground and a robust undercarriage to resist rough landings. It also incorporated an internal skid framework to help protect the crew in the event of a belly landing. 1137 L worth of fuel tanks were located around the center of gravity in the main fuselage to maintain consistent flight characteristics regardless of fuel load. It had a range of 1097 km on internal tanks but this could be extended through the use of two 455L external wingtip tank. Due to the requirement that it be able to operate from older and less prepared runways, the first two Tutor prototype were fitted with retractable air intake screens to protect the engine from ingesting foreign object debris. These screens would cover the intakes during landings, taxing, and take offs, and then retract behind a boundary layer deflector when in flight to minimize drag. These were later scraped on the production version but were brought back with the exported G model. Weapon mounts were included but were not used initially. These consisted of two under-fuselage mount along with four under-wing pylon mounts on each wing. It was intended that combinations of fuel tanks, guns, bombs, or rockets could be carried and deployed in the training role. Later these too would make a come back with the G model. Throughout the design crew safety, crew communication, ease of maintenance were top priorities. However performance was not forgotten. The airframe was designed to take between -3 and +7Gs, this combined with a good rate of climb made the Tutor a solid aerobatic platform. This fact was not lost on the RCAF who mounted smoke generators to the exhaust and went on to use the Toot for most of its display teams. Canadair, and later the RCAF, would perform extensive structural test on the airframes to ensure that they would be able to handle this aerobatic strain safely. Flight testing of the first prototype continued while a second was produced. The second prototype was built to be a stripped down cheaper version of the A model. Canadair intended to make their trainer as appealing as possible to as many foreign countries as possible. The lower cost B model was meant to make it more accessible to less well financed air forces. Modifications included replacing the engine with an Armstrong Siddeley Viper and combining and minimizing the cockpit instruments. The idea was abandoned and the company renewed its focus on the potentially lucrative contracts of providing radar training to the many upcoming F-104 pilots. By 1959, Canadair had begun the slight modification and construction of around 340 F-104 Starfighters. It was this contract that led designers at Canadair to consider the creation of a new lead-in trainer for the high performance jets. The single seat Starfighter and was expected to have low airframe hours requiring an efficient way to transition pilots onto the new type. The company wanted to fill the need for radar and all-weather navigation training and so began incorporating additional systems into the 41s design. The B model was upgraded to the CL-41R standard through extensive airframe modifications and the inclusion of a R24A North American Search and Ranging Radar, or NASARR, and its associated equipment. The redesigned Toot was lengthened by adding a totally new nose section, and had two side blisters along the tail boom to hold the additional equipment required to support the radar set. These blisters also provided a counter-balance for the heavy radar. The engine was upgraded to a Pratt & Whitney JT12A-2. The engine, along with all the extra radar equipment, brought the gross weight up to 3,765kgs, this was heavier than expected and had an impact on performance. The engine was later upgraded again to an Orenda built J85-J4. The performance of the R model was similar to the A but with a slightly reduced top speed and altitude. This model was cancelled due to lack of interest at home and abroad. The role of F-104 radar training for the RCAF was left to three Dakota transports. These were modified to include the NASARR systems and a Starfighter-style nose cone. The CL-41R airframe was then used for vibration and fatigue testing. It now resides in storage at the Reynolds-Alberta Museum, in Wetaskiwin, Alberta. The RCAF was pleased with the Tutor’s performance and they placed an order for 190 aircraft in April 1962. The first production model CL-41A was rolled out of the factory in Montreal on October 29th 1963. The only initial change from the production version was the exclusion of the FOB screens. By 1964, 4 were being produced per month, later a Toot would roll off the production line every 5 days. Several production techniques were used that would later help build the world fastest hydrofoil, the HMCS Bras d'Or, such as mounting the airframes in a jig that reduced workload by easily rotating to any orientation. A modification was proposed during production to correct some of the aerodynamics of the Tutor and improve its spin recovery characteristics. Narrow spin strakes were mounted on either side of the nose to guide airflow at various angles of attack. Almost all the CL-41s were upgraded to include these strakes, the notable exceptions being early production airframes and those used as part of the RCAF’s Golden Centennaires. The CL-41A Tutor, renamed the CT-114 Tutor by the RCAF, entered service on December 16th 1963. Several of the first airframes were attributed to various units for testing and maintenance training. The first operational Tutors were assigned to Training Command at RCAF Gimli in Manitoba and began training pilots in 1963. The first class of pilots graduated in late 1964 and began transitioning to other types. The training program was expanded as more airframes became available. Instructor training was concentrated in Manitoba at the RCAF Portage la Prairie #1 Flying Instructor School. There instructors would familiarise themselves with the aircraft and refine their teaching skills. Primary flight training for student pilots would be concentrated with the #2 Flight Training Squadron, later renamed the #2 Canadian Forces Flying Training School, at RCAF Moose Jaw, in Saskatchewan. The vast majority of Tutors would be based there. The 2 FTS would train all fast jet pilots in Canada as well as a large number of pilots from other countries as part of Canada’s contribution to NATO and other allies. Pilots came from all over the world including Tanzania, Malaysia, Italy, Denmark, Norway, and the Netherlands. The public got to see the new plane in late 1964 at an air show in Toronto. The Tutor was displayed at various other national and international air shows before an air demonstration team could be assembled. The first display team was the Golden Centennaires, formed in 1966 as part of the centennial celebrations. It consisted of 10 modified Tutors painted in a distinctive gold, dark blue and red scheme. They performed from April 28th to November 18th 1967 and was disbanded in January of 1968. In May of 1968 the Red Knight display team was re-equipped with the Tutor following a crash of one of their T-33s. Two airframes were painted red with a white stripe down the fuselage and were used for demonstrations starting in the summer of 1968. Unfortunately a fatal crash in July of 1969 forced an end to the program. Starting in 1976, instructors from the Flying Instructors School based in CFB Moose Jaw began demonstration flights with 4 or 5 unpainted aircraft. They were known as the “Vikings Formation Display Team” and performed at various airshows throughout north america. The group was moved along with the #2 FIS from CFB Moose Jaw to CFB Portage la Prairie and then disbanded in 1984. The most famous Canadian display team is without question the Snowbirds. They were formed in 1969 when they began doing flypasts at Saskatchewan Roughrider football games. These proved to be very popular and so they began touring airshows in 1970. In 1971 they were named the Snowbirds and then in 1975 they officially became the 431 Air Demonstration Squadron, but the old name stuck in the minds of the public. As did the distinctive paint scheme which was introduced between 1974 and 1976. The team operates 11 planes but only uses 9 of them during performances. Snowbird pilots are selected from other squadrons and train together intensively before taking their show on the road. Mistakes and malfunctions can have fatal consequences when planes are this close or closing at such high speeds. Tragically 8 pilots and crew have been killed flying as part of the 431, 4 of which were in front of the public. There have also been several non-fatal crashes. Even with these tragedies the Snowbirds continue to fly and pilots continue to volunteer to participate in such a fantastic tradition of aerial mastery. Canadair had been actively promoting the Tutor to foreign air forces since the late 50s but with little success. The expected orders for the plane from allied air forces were filled by competitors like the Jet Provost and T-37 Tweet. In 1964 the company began to promote a combat version for use in weapons training and light attack roles. The Tutor had been design from the outset to be capable of carrying external stores on 6 pylons, two under the fuselage and two under each wing. A CL-41A was brought back to the Canadair factory and was remade into the CL-41G prototype by adding a reflector gunsight for the pilot, a 16mm gun camera, and strengthened rough field landing gear. Foreign object intake screens were planned but not included on the prototype. The CL-41G began weapons trials in New Brunswick and RCAF Chatham. Further testing was then carried out in California and Florida where the Toot was shown to be able to deliver a large diversity of weapons with great accuracy. The weapons tested include bombs, 2.75 inch and 5 inch rockets, 7.62 mm gun pods, napalm, cluster bombs, and fire bombs. The plane was shown to representatives from the US, mexico, Ecuador, Columbia, Australia and New Zealand, but they showed little interest as many similar platforms were on offer. However Canadair did find some success in an unlikely place. The Malaysian air force was interested in a combat version for use as a counter-insurgency and interdiction aircraft as well as as a trainer. Canadair modified the 41A according to Malaysian specifications and created the CL-41G Tebuan, which is Malay for Wasp. 20 copies were produced between 1966 and 1967 with deliveries starting in July of 1967. The Tebuan saw combat against counter-state militias in the jungles near the border with Thailand. None were lost in combat but several later crashed on various training missions as the plane aged. The last of the Tebuan’s were retired from Malaysian service in 1986. A civil version was also considered. This would have been used either as civilian pilot training or modified with an expanded cockpit to hold four passengers. It was intended that this model be used for high speed executive transport but the concept was not pursued due to lack of interest and possible competition in this category from the Cessna T-37A Tweet, renamed the Cessna Model 407. Like the civil version, the following versions were never pursued past the pencil and paper stage but showed the great potential of the little Tutor. The CL-41J was a proposed VTOL version that incorporated three small jet engines behind the canopy. A large door covered the engines when the plane was in forward flight. The CL-41K was meant to be low level reconnaissance version. It mounted 3 Vinten cameras in the nose along side a terrain following radar. The CL-41N series was intended to be a US carrier trainer and sported reinforced landing gear and a strengthened rear fuselage to support an arresting hook. The CL-41T would have been a complete redesign for ground attack and troop support missions. It had two engines, terrain following radar, and 8 external hardpoints for weapons and fuel tanks. By the 1980s Canadian Tutor’s were beginning to show their age. In 1980 the glass windshields of the Tutors were replaced with those made from polycarbonate. This was done to increase the Tutor’s resiliency with respect to bird strikes. While this somewhat modernised the Toot, further modifications were required. Communication equipment and other additional systems were needed for its training mission. In addition to this the aircrafts avionics were aging and could no longer be supported. An upgrade program was put in place in 1985 to extend the life of the hard working jet past the turn of the century. VHF communication equipment was installed to bring the Tutor up to compliance with commercial aircraft flying into civilian airports. The Tutor also received a UHF transceiver, dual channel intercoms, solid state power inverters, and a new blade-style antenna behind the cockpit canopy. Even with these upgrades the systems onboard the Tutors was starting to age. After 37 years of service the Tutor was largely retired from service with the RCAF in 2000. The notable exceptions being those still flying with the Snowbirds. The airframes were retired to RCAF Mountain View, in Ontario where they joined a mothballed air force. This abundance of lower hour airframes is one of the reasons the Snowbirds have been able to continue flying for so long. They were replaced by CT-156 Harvard II and the CT-155 Hawk as the RCAF’s primary jet Canadair moved on from the success of their CL-41 and produce other successful in house designs like the swing-wing CL-84 Dynavert, and the CL-89 and CL-289 surveillance drones. It would also continue to modify and produce many aircraft for the Canadian armed forces such as the supersonic CF-5, which would provide both light attack and advanced training roles. The humble Tutor, or Toot as it was affectionately called, trained every fast aircraft pilot in the RCAF from the mid-1960 through past the turn of the century. But it wasn’t just Canadians who benefited from its outstanding training qualities, the Tutor trained hundreds of pilots from other NATO and friendly countries. During its service the Tutor was involved with 67 accidents resulting in the destruction of 65 airframes and the deaths of 22 pilots and students. Today only the snowbirds continue to fly the jet and as the airframes age and fewer remain available, they too will eventually retire one of Canada’s most iconic jets.

- Snowbird - Hidden History Unveiled: Kind: captions Language: en In this video, we're going to go over the history of Snowbird and how it came to be one of the premier skiing destinations in America. Carved into the granite circs of Utah's Little Cottonwood Canyon, Snowbird is more than just a ski resort, it is an experience, a challenge, and a testament to a singular vision realized against formidable odds. Its story is one of audacious dreams and engineering marvels, transforming a rugged landscape once prospected by miners into a world-renowned bastion of steep and deep skiing. All starting with the relentless vision of one man. Long before the first chairlift turned, the story of Little Cottonwood Canyon was written in silver. In 1869, a US Army soldier's discovery of silver ore sparked a mining boom, giving rise to operations like the famed Emma mine. But as the silver rush faded, the canyon grew quiet, waiting for its next chapter. That chapter began in the mind of Ted Johnson, a former ski instructor and manager at the neighboring Alta Lodge. Johnson, a graceful skier and former surfer nicknamed the Silver Fox, spent his free time exploring the vast untamed terrain of Peruvian Gulch and Gad Valley. He saw potential not for just another ski area, but for a yearround European style mountain resort with modern amenities and aerial tramways scaling the imposing peaks. To realize this dream, Johnson began the arduous process of acquiring the land, painstakingly purchasing old mining claims. However, vision without capital is merely a dream. Johnson spent years trying to secure financing, but the sheer scale of his proposal and the unforgiving nature of the canyon made investors wary. The turning point came in 1969 when Johnson met Richard Dick Bass at a party in Vale, Colorado. A wealthy Texas oilman, rancher, and passionate mountaineer, Bass was immediately captivated by Johnson's vision. He became the majority owner, providing the financial engine the project desperately needed. With Johnson staying on as the resort's first president and general manager, the duo formed a dynamic partnership. And in 1970, construction began at a furious pace. The centerpiece of the entire development was the aerial tram. In an era when most ski areas were built around chairlifts, the decision to install a massive 125 passenger tram was a statement of intent. Manufactured by Gavventa of Switzerland, the tram was an engineering feat of the highest order, costing 3.5 million on its own. It was designed to ascend 2,900 vertical feet from the base area at 8,100 ft to the windswept 11,000 ft summit of Hidden Peak in a single breathtaking ride. The construction was a battle against the elements with Swiss crews working in harsh conditions, reportedly nicknaming the site Snow Hell. They allegedly smuggled 200 gallons of schnops into Utah, labeled as cable grease, to keep their spirits high while helicopters carefully airlifted the massive steel towers into place on the steep rocky slopes. Parallel to the tram's construction was the development of the base village with an architectural style as bold as the peaks, brutalism. The local architectural firm Brixen and Christopher understood that any structure in Little Cottonwood Canyon had to withstand immense snow loads and the constant threat of avalanches. The thick boardformed concrete walls of the tram building were designed for resilience, creating a unique aesthetic that appeared to erupt from the granite bedrock itself. On December 23rd, 1971, Snowbird officially opened to the public. On that day, skiers had access to the brand new aerial tram, the Wilbur chairlift in Provian Gulch, and the Gad 1 and Gad 2 chairlifts in the adjacent Gad Valley. The resort's reputation was forged almost instantly, cementing its status as an expert's paradise. Throughout the 1970s, Snowbird focused on refining its operations. The original Cliff Lodge opened in 1973, followed by the Iron Blossom Lodge in 1974. The resort continued to grow, adding the Peruvian Double Chairlift in 1976. This decade was about establishing a foothold and learning to manage the canyon's complexities, including its legendary snowfall, averaging over 500 in of light, dry powder annually. The canyon is one of North America's most active avalanche zones. The daily boom of the howitzer used for avalanche control by the ski patrol became the canyon's morning relevant. The 1980s brought transformative growth that realized the founders's vision for a worldclass yearround destination. Completed in 1986, a massive expansion of the Cliff Lodge added 352 new rooms, a full service spa, and extensive conference facilities, making it the largest destination ski hotel in the world at the time. This was crucial for attracting a broader clientele and establishing a summer business. This period also coincided with Dick Bass's personal pursuit of the Seven Summits. On April 30th, 1985, he became the first person to climb the highest peak on each continent. An adventurous spirit that perfectly mirrored the bold ethos of the resort he had built. As Snowbird entered the 1990s, the focus shifted to modernizing the skier experience. The old Gad one double chair was converted into the high-speed Gad Zoom quad in 1997. And in 1995, the Baby Thunder lift created a muchneeded learning area. The decade's most innovative project, however, was a tunnel. To access the expansive bowls of mineral basin on the back side of Hidden Peak, the resort board a 600 ft long tunnel straight through the granite ridge line. Inside, they installed a conveyor belt, creating North America's only ski tunnel. In May 2014, Dick Bass sold his majority interest to the family, founders of Powder Corporation. Bass remained as chairman of the board, but the transaction marked the end of an era. The passing of Dick Bass in July 2015 at the age of 85 was a poignant farewell to the larger than-l life figure whose spirit was woven into the fabric of the mountain. Perhaps the most ambitious project of this era was the realization of a dream dating back to the founders. In the summer of 2015, the Summit Restaurant opened a top Hidden Peak. This 23,000q foot two-story facility, part of a $35 million capital investment, offered 360 degree views and upscale dining at 11,000 ft. As Snowbird looked to the future, it continued to invest while honoring its past. In 2024, the resort announced the replacement of the original Wilbear double chair, another opening day lift from 1971 named for co-founder Ted Johnson's wife, Wilma. The plan for a new fixed grip quad balanced modern efficiency with a nostalgic nod to the resort's storied past. From Ted Johnson's first surveys of mining claims to the opening of a new frontier through a tunnel in the mountain, Snowbird has masterfully navigated a period of maturation and modernization. It has evolved from a raw experts onlyly mountain into a globally recognized destination. All without sacrificing the core identity that makes it a truly great mountain. That's it for this week's leisure lore. And please do us a favor and subscribe to this channel if you enjoyed it. It means the world to us.

- CAF Story | From a Cessna 172 with Dad to a Snowbird CT-114 Tutor: Kind: captions Language: en The first plane I flew was a Cessna 172 and I was so short that I was sitting on my dad’s lap, and basically I was just going up and down. That’s all I did. Both my parents were pilots, just as a hobby. It was a very passion that they had and they totally transmitted that to me from an early age. So, I’m Captain Sarah Dallaire, Snowbird 2 with the Canadian Forces Snowbirds. I’m in the inner right wing position, and I’m from Lévis, Quebec. I think what happened with me when I was younger, I was so wanting to do everything. So I was in figure skating five times a week, I was in Air Cadets, we had that on Wednesday and on the weekends, and plus going to school. And just, you know, dirt biking at 14 years old that I started doing every day. It just got so busy that I think my interests were everywhere and I was trying to get all the skills that I could get. Well, I love everything that requires mental preparation and visualization, I totally love. That’s what I get hooked on. In the Air Force we call it ‘chair flying’. Before, in the dirt bike, I didn’t call it anything, but I just was doing it and my dad was really good at kind of teaching me to close my eyes, visualize all the jumps, all the turns. And I think it’s exactly the same in an airplane. The more you can rehearse on the ground, the more you can fly, obviously, you get kind of ahead of the plane. I’m actually thrilled to go meet the public because what I haven’t told you yet is, when I was twelve I saw Maryse Carmichael, the first female Snowbird pilot and she literally put a little flame into my heart that I hold onto it, I held onto it all my life. So when I was selected to be a Snowbird pilot she was there with me and it was the greatest, one of my greatest moments for sure as a pilot. And so now to get the chance to go meet other young women, or even you know anybody that wants to follow their dream, I think one can make a difference for sure. You know when you say you want to be a pilot and somebody says no, that could have ended there. For me to see Maryse Carmichael that year was very special, in that she’s a woman, and wow, she can do it so I want to do it. You know it just made more, an even more concrete goal for me. If I would have looked back 10 years or 15 years saying I was going to be a Snowbird pilot, it’s way too big of a goal. You need to break it down and it just will happen if you keep it in mind. - Digital Snowbird Lifestyle: Work 9 to 5 While Traveling the World | 21 Stephanie Zummo: Kind: captions Language: en I want to be home for Christmas 'cause that's when I get to see my whole family. armed and ready the men hastily rushed outside only for a third gunshot to disrupt the Stillness patriarch Billy then solemnly conveyed to the cameras that the life they're living isn't worth dying for sending a shiver down the viewers spines however Anchorage Daily News revealed a curious twist to this tale as Alaskan state troopers tasked with maintaining Law and Order in the region reported that no individual associated with Alaskan Bush People people seem to have reported any shots fired this in congruity raised legitimate questions about the authenticity of the show's dramatic moments that their Wilderness escapades may be more intermittent than portrayed certain onscreen relationships have also been called into question such as Noah's supposed date Karina Kaufman revealed to be an actress thus casting doubt on the authenticity of the show's romantic entanglements in this case it's quite possible that the production team saw it necessary to raise the apparent attraction of they weren't living in State this wasn't their first legal entanglement either having faced charges on sworn falsification and theft in 2014 accused of dishonesty about their residency to secure Snowbird stressed that Ammy isn't a lot better yet, but she is improving and the family is grateful for every step forward. But she's on the upswing, and being back in a home environment will likely help her feel stronger than being stuck in a hospital room. For newer viewers, Snowbird has become one of the most recognizable members of the Brown family thanks to Discovery Channel's Alaskan Bush people, which followed the clan's off-thegrid lifestyle and their close-knit bond. Over the years, Bird has often shared heartfelt direct to camera updates, especially when it comes to family milestones and health scares. Amy's health journey has been a major part of the family's story for nearly a decade. In 2017, she was diagnosed with advanced lung cancer, and her illness prompted major changes for the Browns, including relocating so she could receive treatment. By 2018, Ammy announced she was in remission. But the years since have still included ongoing health challenges and repeated medical scares, including serious respiratory illnesses that have sent her back to the hospital more than once. Just days before Snowbird's good news update, Amy's son, Bear, also told followers that Ammy wasn't feeling well and was taken in for medical care as a precaution due to symptoms like fever and congestion. A scary situation given her history. Now with Ammy back out of the hospital and on her way home, Snowbird's message landed exactly the way she intended, not as a victory lap, but as a hopeful breath in the middle of a tough season and a reminder that the Browns are still leaning on faith, family, and their fans when it matters most.
