  8. @Djsearsusa01 [3 likes]: Several observations from a couple of retired American living in Canada who have friends on family both sides of the border.  1. The exchange rate is not a significant factor…the exchange rate compared to 2013 overlooks that the 10 year $0.76. The figure your realtor noted is cherry picking an uncharacteristically high rate.  2. The Canadian dollar was near par in the mid 70s but dropped over the subsequent decades to fluctuate in the $0.82 to $0.85 range. January the rate has decreased 5-8% from November 2024.  3. Many properties bought by Canadians in the last 5 years were done when the exchange was nowhere near the figure noted by the realtor….likely purchased in the 0.80-0.83 range.   4. The exchange rate often noted by the American tourist industry as a major factor overlooks that Canadians since last spring have been traveling to Europe, Asia and South America in record numbers. Many Canadians snowbirds who normally winter in the US are going instead to,Spain, Portugal, Mexico, Panama, the Dominican Republic, and other Caribbean Islands. We have elected ourselves with my sister and her husband to winter in Spain.  Another family member has elected to buy a condo in the Dominican Republic for retirement rather than South Carolina or Florida as was there plan a year ago.  

5. Some Canadians who have property will continue to own and use them, others will own them and elect to rent them Americans while they winter elsewhere.   6. Whenever the Canadian drops for an extended period, Canadian wages and savings have adjusted along with them resulting in the purchasing power ultimately becoming the same.   

7.  The President’s repeatedly saying he wants Canada and his 51 State comments have quickened Canadians to their core.  There is a renewed sense of nationalism and pride.  I was shocked by the number of Quebecois (nationalist Quebecers) who use to describe themselves as Quebecois, Canadian second, are calling themselves Canadians from Quebec…this is a huge shift in thinking.  8. For decades Canadians saw themselves as American cousins with subtle differences. Now the shift is towards viewing the relationship as neighbors who they wave to and say hello, but with mixed feelings about visiting.   

9. The desire to be friends remains, but Canadians by and large are pushing their elected leaders to decouple as much as possible from the US. I don’t think the relationship will return to what it was in the past….both economically, socially, militarily enmeshed for decades. They are pushing for building infrastructure that will see more of our aluminum, gas, oil, minerals, wood, agricultural products and other manufactured products to Asia, Europe and South America. 

The feelings are akin to a couple on the verge of separating and heading towards an angry divorce if further missteps occur.

Those American tourist officials who fail to understand the mind shift and anger of the average Canadian and think it is mainly due to the economy and exchange rates will conclude it is a momentary issue.  Better to recognize the heart of the issue, and adjust to having far fewer Canadian snowbirds and tourists for the next 5 years, possibly longer.

Wayne Smith is a professor at the Ted Rogers School of Hospitality and Tourism Management, director of the Institute for Hospitality and Tourism Research at Toronto Metropolitan University. What do we know about how significant the drop is in terms of Canadians not heading to the United States? >> There's a number that everyone seems to be ignoring. There was a big report done with the US, all the northern states that just put out. Two of the states recorded the credit card spending >> and both of them, Montana and Vermont were down over 40 to almost 50% in credit card spending. And that's the real number because people look at the border numbers going across. Well, that counts everyone who's going across for work or crossing for, you know, transport trucks and stuff like that. When you look at the credit card numbers and we're seeing spending down between 40 and 50%. That is showing that it's having a real impact. I mean, both of our guests, Joan and, uh, earlier in in the conversation that we had, um, were talking about the fact that they agree that the the comments from Donald Trump are egregious, that they're upset by by what the president has said, and yet they are also, you know, split on on what they're doing. Some pe people are going, some people aren't going. What is your sense as to why folks aren't making that trip, who aren't going down across the border? >> Tourism at its heart is a safety industry. You have to feel like you're going to be safe at the destination you're you're going to. And different people have different levels of safety threshold, right? So some people will walk down a certain street and think, "Oh, this is perfectly fine." And some people say, "Oh, I don't want to go down that street because it's dangerous." You know? So some people look at, you know, for example, crossing the border if they have a threshold and then they're worried about, you know, going to secondary inspection and all those things or worried about the ICE or worried about the not following all the rules, the new rules about staying more than 30 days and getting the fingerprinting and stuff like that. You know, different people will have very different thresholds and you're seeing that right now. >> Do you think that I mean those new rules in particular about if you're staying for more than 30 days, has has that turned some people off? >> I think it definitely has. I think the whole idea that when you're crossing, you want to feel like you're part of a community, right? So, when you're crossing and all of a sudden we're going to fingerprint you and we're going to, you know, do the biometrics and take out your picture, it for some people makes them feel like they've done something wrong even though they've done nothing wrong. And just that feeling, it creates that insecurity which creates a feeling that am I welcome here? >> What does it mean for the local economies? I mean, again, this goes back to the credit card data that you're talking about. We've heard stories of Vegas, for example, the number of Canadians who aren't going to Las Vegas and spending all sorts of money. Those border states as well, but also down in places like Florida and Arizona and Palm Springs, where a lot of Canadians would go to escape the cold weather. What does that mean for their economies? Well, the big news you're going to see is come February when they're starting to work on next year's budgets, all of a sudden all those taxes that they're getting from the accommodations tax, the food preparation tax, the the different taxes, the admissions taxes, all those revenues, the sales taxes in Buffalo, for example, would be another one. They take a big portion of that to help run those v those cities. So the US tax system is different than the Canadian one where a lot of those tourism taxes actually fund a lot of local and state services. So when you take away that tax base, all of a sudden you're looking at there's been estimates in Orlando, for example, that 29% of the property tax is saved by the tourism taxes. >> So you're not going to raise taxes 29% on property taxes. So what are you going to do? >> Something's going to be cut. Exactly. >> Yeah. Joan talked about the fact that when she's down in Florida, people apologize to her, that they feel as though they need to say something. What is your sense of that? That that relationship that people have um in the places if they are choosing to go, the relationship that they have with the people in those communities >> on an individual basis, people are nice just like anywhere else, right? You can go somewhere and you get individual relationships and when I'm looking face to face with someone, you know, we're going to be kind. we're going to be nice to one another and civil most of the time. But what happens is is when you're looking at the percentage of people who take Florida for example, you had more than 50% of the people vote for Trump and they knew sort of what he's all about. They had a whole first term with them. I lived in the states during the first Trump term so I experienced it. So, you know, they knew that what they were getting into, but they still voted for it. So, it's sort of an interesting kind of dichotomy there that you're seeing. Charlie's not taking his trailer to Texas. He is going to Mexico instead. Um, where are Canadians going? If they're not going, if we aren't going to Florida and Arizona and what have you, where are we going instead? >> Mexico is a big location. You're also seeing some South American countries like Colombia and some of the areas around there would be work. You're also seeing the Dominican Republic. You're also, if you're going to Europe, Portugal is the big winner. seems to be the big winner for Canadian tourists. >> And you wonder whether people are just staying here as well. I mean, again, we talk about escaping the winter. Winter's a lovely thing. I mean, if you embrace it, if you lean into it, um, this country is beautiful in the winter. Is your sense that people are spending, we talk about this, but are people spending more money in this country now? >> We haven't seen the numbers quite yet for obviously this winter. We're seeing some really good reports out of like Whistle Blackholm and a couple of those other places that their numbers seem to be rising. We haven't seen anything official yet, but I I think you're going to see places like Victoria, for example, do very well this year where people can go there, they can escape the snow, but and have temperate weather at the same time. I I think you're going to see some places like that do really really well. How long-term do you think this will be? Um it feels like I mean the prime minister has talked about the relationship between Canada and the United States being different right now. Um Charlie is looking for in some ways a different administration but also a different attitude if he can put it that way. So how long is this going to play out do you think? >> This is really interesting because what happens is you're having people break habits. >> So he was talking about he's been going down 12 to 15 years. Well if he goes down to Mexico and has a great experience what's stop him from going in that's becoming your new habit. So, as consumers, we're very we like to be buying the same thing over and over and over again. If we get a brand we really like, we tend to buy it all the time. It's the same type of idea where you're setting new travel patterns and new travel ideas. So, this summer, instead of those Quebec visitors going to Old Orchard, well, they're now going to Halifax. Well, they had a great Halifax experience, what's to stop them from going back to Halifax next year instead of Old Orchard? So you're if you can this is an opportunity for these destinations to really establish new habits, new customers and bring them in and if they treat them well, if they give them good value for money, this they could be developing a new customer they could have for another decade. >> Treating them well is key. As you said earlier, tourism and travel so much is about feeling comfortable, feeling like you're welcomed there. >> Exactly. So when you get into these experiences, it's imperative that you get this sense I am a guest. See, the the biggest thing that's turning off a lot of the Canadian market is when they hear the US politicians start talking about, oh, we're losing all this money. No one wants to be thought of as a dollar sign. You know, no one wants to be thought of, oh, we just miss you because you're spending with us. It's the totally wrong attitude that we're seeing. >> So that is turning off a lot of people in itself. Have you rethought where you're traveling because of what we're talking about? >> You know, I spent I'm originally from Toronto. I spent 15 years in the States. I have really good friends in Charleston, South Carolina where I spent 15 years and a couple of them are in their 70s. I'd love to go see them. And I keep going back and forth whether or not I would I would go or not. I'm leaning to not, but you know, if something ever happened to one of them, [snorts] you know, they're my friends. They're my family. And it's really tough to say no to that. It's a personal decision for so many people. Wayne, thank you. Thank you so much. Wayne Smith, professor at the Ted Rogers School of Hospitality and Tourism Toronto Metropolitan University. You've been listening to the current podcast. My name is Matt Galloway. Thanks for listening. I'll talk to you soon.

Cherokees did indeed live in wooden houses rather than the tepes of the Plains Indian by the early 1800s a few Cherokees in Northwest Georgia were living like wealthy whites as inke Keepers and even as slaveholding plantation owners Chief James vanan was both this was his Tavern once a popular way station for whites on the frontier it was near his Trading Post on the kosuga river and this was his home half white and known for his explosive and violent temper van owned over a hundred black slaves and once burned alive one who escaped forcing other slaves to witness the murder in 1809 van himself was murdered in a brawl the financial success of Cherokees like van and their ability to excel in business Pursuits was a greater threat to White settlers than their fears real and imagined of Indian savagery during the 1820s not far from the van Plantation in Northwest Georgia the Cherokees created a new town new aota constant losses of their tribal lands at the hands of whites LED them to create a Cherokee Nation from the remaining lands whose Center had been pushed 200 miles Southwest from the Carolinas new aota was designed to be the capital of this new nation it was a determined effort to prove to the whites that they were quite capable of handling their own Affairs this new formal nation was even modeled after the United States with a constitution a legisl and a supreme court and we were powerful we were very powerful we were very intell ENT you know we had our own language schools newspapers everything and we we were always very intelligent being Cherokee today is very hard who Who Am I who really am I am I Cherokee or am I am I am I not Cherokee am I a non- Indian what's going to be the basis of my decision here which way am I going to go I still have struggles with the mound ceremony is an ancient Cherokee right when my people came to meet for an important occasion everyone brought a turtle shell of dirt from their home and added it to the SA fire Mound as the ceremony ended over time the meeting Mound grew with the power of past meetings in Snowbird this ceremony now opens the annual fading voices ceremony which commemorates the community's elders and celebrates Cherokee ways and traditions we have a little far going and it's made of different types of farwood and the Seven Trips that we make around the mound represents the Seven Clans the clan system has largely died Out Among the Eastern band but like most Native American Clans it once maintained a matrilineal social system where family lineages were passed on through mothers rather than fathers and members were forbidden to marry within their clan on special occasions like the fading voices Gathering Cherokee women and make traditional foods like Hony corn Chestnut bread and bean dumplings flute making is another traditional craft being revived here in Snowbird by Billy Welsh who finds a demand for his flutes in the Galleries and shops of ball playing is an Old Cherokee sport which is still played during the annual Fall Festival held every year in Cherokee it was once a major Cherokee social occasion where young men could demonstrate their physical daring stamina and skill like all elements of Cherokee life traditional ball playing had spiritual meaning and once required intense spiritual preparation and purification including sexual abstinence and a scratching ceremony to purify the blood for each player prior to every game ball playing was once used to settle disputes within the tribe when I was going up they would have a land dispute or something like that they go to Ballfield back then when they have a land dispute that's how they settle ball in the 19th century Cherokee students at Mission School schools were often forbidden to even attend ball plays for fear that they would be corrupted by the gambling drinking coarse language and the fact that players were quote practically naked another Cherokee tradition fading but still held by some concerns the existence of little people small bands of elusive elf-sized beings who live in the mountains and sometimes come in contact with both children and adults all I've ever heard about people they related to them as being in the mountains or the woods and I've always heard of people having little people as far as cheres and that including everybody all the cheres that little you know involved with the people and and part of the society as well as just part of the church Samuel wer was 27 years old when he came to new aota in 1825 from Boston for the American Board of Commissioners for foreign missions this was his home in the Cherokee Capital where he helped lead the fight against removal he was arrested by the Georgia guard in 1831 and imprisoned for 18 months for refusing to take an oath of allegiance to the state of Georgia a strong supporter of the Cherokee Nation he and another missionary found themselves at the center of a National Power struggle between the states and the federal government Georgia refused to recognize Cherokee claims to their land while John Marshall Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court did former Indian fighter and now President Andrew Jackson whose life had once been saved in the Creek Indian War by Cherokee Chief junaluska chose to ignore the Court's ruling Jackson wrote to a friend the decision of the court has fell stillborn when gold had been discovered several years earlier on Cherokee land Georgia immediately forbade Indian access to it when Cherokee claims on Georgia land were no longer was still occupied by the Cherokees themselves if you would be misbehaving or doing something that you shouldn't be doing or saying things that you shouldn't be saying that some times the um the - South Georgia - Penguin Paradise of the South Atlantic | Free Documentary Nature: Kind: captions Language: en not far from antarctica a vast mountainous island looms exposed to the storms of the southern ocean inhospitable and hostile but still an annual destination for millions of penguins [Music] for their chicks the first summer is crucial and perilous enough while winter presents the greatest challenge of their lives year after year they return to south georgia the city of penguins one of the largest animal gatherings on earth far from europe between the southern tips of south africa and south america and only 1 000 kilometers north of the antarctic the island of south georgia towers up out of the sea it's the very top of an underwater mountain range as big as the spanish island of majorca september late winter on south georgia winds of up to 180 kilometers an hour temperatures under -15 degrees celsius for months thousands of king penguin chicks have been waiting for their parents in a vast juvenile colony in winter the penguins fishing grounds lie far away from the island it won't be long before the youngster's last fat reserves are used up if the parents don't get back in time the little ones will starve the first breeding birds have already gathered on an old whaling ship south georgia shags live here the whole year round they build their nests in ice and snow breeding early in the year so the offspring are fully fledged by next winter in contrast to the shags king penguins take a very long time to reach adulthood they only start fishing at one and a half years old for now they go to kindergarten without supervision however at the end of winter the first elephant seals are appearing at the coast there's still enough living space but that's going to change pretty soon a good spot on the beach guarantees female attention whoever already has one defends it ferociously in these contests over seven tons meet each other head to head as many as 60 females make up a harem the bull assertively puts the newcomer to flight when the elephant seals occupy the beaches it's also time for the penguins to get back to their young [Applause] even the bigger chicks are reaching the end of their strength not all of them survive the long winter a skewer mingles among the exhausted youngsters for him there's plenty of food around these days the chicks try to keep the predator at bay only their parents return can save the young birds from starvation now finally the time has come the deserted winter beaches are stormed by summer guests in the coming months two million king penguins will crowd the island they head straight for their offspring without delay one couple find each other again on the beach after months at sea greetings are kept short they still have quite a journey ahead rush hour in a snowstorm their bellies are full of fish finally the penguin parents reach the colony of kids parents and youngsters recognize each other in the crowd by their calls even after months apart the long wait was worth it from now on parents provide a steady food delivery service well into summer only then will their offspring fish in the ocean themselves others are less fortunate the couple from the beach have been searching for hours to find their chick but their calls remain unanswered the voice of their youngster has been silenced forever oh a small consolation remains for the parents they can start breeding earlier now and so prepare the new offspring better for the following winter in october the days are getting longer and the sun's stronger snow and ice retreat back to the mountain summits up to 3 000 metres high around half of the island lies permanently under ice and snow there are more than 160 glaciers here many of them slide down as far as the sea the overland journey of the ice ends here after thousands of years on the move from now on it will drift along in the southern ocean in the form of ice flows last summer's chicks are now enjoying the spring sunshine and the all-inclusive service package delivered by their parents they're well fed and already plucking the baby down feathers from their plumage not only the youngest ones are shedding feathers before the penguins can breed again they have to molt for several weeks the estuary of an icy glacial river provides a perfect spot king penguins change their plumage every year old feathers are no good for insulation anymore and are shed one by one penguin metabolisms are working flat out now and body temperatures are rising the birds need to cool down the young penguin couple occupies a desirable patch of cold water the penguins coats are itching all over while the penguins wait for their new even more summer visitors arrive at the island gen 2 penguins are fast swimmers but only reach the south georgian coast in november plots with direct access to the sea are already taken thankfully gen 2 penguins prefer green spaces with scenic views higher up in the mountains they make their way up a steep path through a veritable jungle of tussock grass the strenuous ascent is rewarded with a majestic view up here they'll raise a new generation of gentoo penguins macaroni penguins also arrive in droves more than five million of them flock to the island their territory is along the rocky shoreline of south georgia these gold-crested birds are smaller than the gen 2 and king penguins but they're extremely tough as keen climbers they're not at all bothered by the minimalist state of their summer residence they favor the barren rocky areas after all the plentiful fishing grounds are right on their doorstep back in the beaches the molting of the king penguins is over the huge crowds gradually disperse leaving behind a sea of feathers the young couple has also got a brand new outfit they retreat to the safety of the colony who would dare to lay an egg with these neighbors around it's a good one and a half kilometers to walk from the coast to penguin city a long way for short legs from a vantage point they get a first impression of the lie of the land as in all big cities location is everything successful breeding can depend on it but it's very early season the property market is still quiet the couple have quickly found a suitable plot a brief glance at the colony shows that public displays of love are no easy matter there's always someone interfering the exchange of affections leads to a skillful balancing act in just a few days the female will lay her single precious egg in contrast to the emperor penguins of the antarctic mainland whose fathers incubate the eggs in winter alone king penguins take turns each it all looks so well established and untouched but the incomparable animal world of south georgia has a tragic history it began in the 19th century with seal hunting and came to a climax with industrial whaling the island whaling stations were in operation for about 60 years today only ruins and wreckage remind us of a time in which the sea ran red with the blood of thousands of whales in the early 20th century margarine soap and nitroglycerine for weapons were produced from whale oil the discovery of a vast whale population led to a true gold rush grease from whale blubber was the basis of the industrial revolution and became very expensive whalers lived and worked in six stations here processing 175 000 whales in 60 years they erected buildings on penguin breeding grounds bringing with them domestic animals and rats the workers made omelettes from penguin eggs and showed little respect for nature only in 1965 did the whalers leave south georgia for good and the animals could once again reign supreme on the island millions of antarctic fur seals were doomed due to their warm coats only a tiny colony survived the relentless human slaughter more than three million of them live on south georgia today the world's largest community of marine mammals sheltered - First Language - The Race to Save Cherokee: Kind: captions Language: en Captioning provided by: Quickcaption, Inc. www.quickcaption.com ♪♪ [rushing water] >> WaaaHoooo! [distant cheering] [Cherokee phrase] they say [Cherokee word] The ball was picked up by the hand and it is called [Cherokee word] A lot of Cherokee language was used and it was all used in the games when I played back in the 50s and 60s on both teams, both sides. Whether it's Bird Town or Wolf Town or Paint Town, we all spoke the Cherokee language and we talked Cherokee in a ball game. (Speaking in Cherokee). >> They did speak in Cherokee, mostly all of them, way back when I was growing up. There wasn't too many people that speak in English, just a few of them. And you'd go to the home and they'd all speak in Cherokee, everywhere you went. And now you can't go nowhere and they'd say, "I don't know how to speak it." >> We were not allowed to speak our language in school, we were banned from it. And that was that come from the federal government. They had their ways of punishing us if we spoke if we were caught speaking the language. >> Growing up I heard stories from my grandparents about the boarding school and the push for assimilation. Grandpa said he learned enough English in the first six months to keep from getting a whipping every day. And but they'd only use Cherokee at home. And so it was traumatic for him. >> I'm not so sure that language was the only reason that the children were encouraged to speak English and not Cherokee. I think it probably had a lot to do with Americanizing the Cherokee children. >> When we were growing up when, we were real young that was all that they talked was the Cherokee language, very little English. Even my mother she was she had a hard time pronouncing a lot of English words. And she talked to us in the Cherokee language, but there was a bigger interest in the English because that's the way we were living. >> I remember in the early 60s as a little girl going to a community meeting and there was some people that came from the government, the BIA, and came to our community meeting and they were telling our parents how important it was that their children learn English. It was going to better their lives and they were going to be able to go to town and get a job. So I think because our parents wanted what was good for us or better things for us, they encouraged us to speak English. >> People would tell us, you know, you have to learn the white man's way and that's even including the language, because they'd say this is a white man's world and you're going to be going out and you're going to have to learn some of the things. Had they said, it's okay if you speak English, but you need to keep talking Cherokee, but they didn't they just thought, well, it's the best thing for our kids, so they encouraged us I think it's that time period that we started losing >> Mine and Shirley's generation is probably the last and I'll be 59 in October and so will Shirley be 59 in November. And I think we're it. >> This would have been the heart of the Cherokee world. The Cherokee world kind of goes out from this place in almost a spider web or a wagon wheel, kind of. And we know that this is so deep into the Cherokee world that it is unlikely that you would have had contact with other tribal people coming in here. So, in essence, think of Kituwah as Vatican City with Rome around it. So your trade, your international trade would have been at places like Keowee, Naquasi. Just over those mountains right there, that's where you would have had, you know, all the languages you could imagine being spoken at, but here, this would have been purely Cherokee, purely Cherokee ceremonies taking place here, purely Cherokee stories taking place here. Traditionally, elders and story tellers tell us that Cherokees have been here since the beginning; the creator put them here specifically. Archeologically and anthropologically we believe that Cherokees have been here for at least 12,000 years. >> A lot of these Cherokees that lived here lived in Keowee and they had reservations, private reservations in 1819 and North Carolina come in and said, well, you made that treaty with the United States you didn't make that treaty with North Carolina, so they forced them out. So imagine every five or ten years you've got to move and you've got to pack up whatever you can put on your back and you're leaving everything else at home, everything. That's what those Cherokees had to go through year after year after year after year and you're still here. >> In the beginning, there were only, I'll say 1200, I hear a different number, of how many people escaped the Trail of Tears removal. All those people spoke the Cherokee language in those days, 1200 of them. And today we have 14, 000 and only 200, I'll say 200, speak the language. I don't know, it might be more it might be even less than 200 that speak the language. >> I haven't heard the "help me" word today. [Speaking Cherokee] >> There's a few fluent speakers, not too many, but those of us who can almost speak it try to incorporate it and use it more in our daily lives to preserve it. The spirit of getting together and working together on crafts brings out the desire to use the language. It just feels more natural. We cannot teach the children during these next two weeks everything that there is to know about beadwork or basketry or river cane or white oak, but we also know that as with the corn beads, the most important thing is to plant the seed and that's what we're doing. We're planting the seed of beadwork and basketry and being able to share what little we know about the Cherokee language we're because just during the first part of the week Jim introduced the word (speaking in Cherokee) to them when they need help they're to say and now they're using it frequently on their own. And whatever little bit we can help them and guide them with, that's what we're all about. [speaking Cherokee] >> We have this summer language camp that we do every summer and it's for six weeks. They're here at 8:00 in the morning, just like going to school. So if they were just looking for something to do, they wouldn't be here. >> It's a little bit hard sometimes, because you get new students every year. So that's why we're going back and we do the colors over, we do the numbers and the syllabary is just an ongoing thing. Because if the children who learn it one year, you know, they're not going to read it the rest of the year, maybe, so when they come back they have to be refreshed. And it's just a course to introduce them to the language and perhaps and, maybe, you know, what we pray is that they'll be so interested in it that they'll want to learn it. >> We know that the new generation that's coming up that's not learning Cherokee, they're kind of stuck in the middle. Part of them is native and part of them is trying to survive in this world. They have to play by everybody else's rules, you know, and try to live in a society that doesn't make sense to them, because even if they can't speak the language, they still are hanging on to their culture, a piece of their culture as much as they can, but they don't understand it, you know? And to understand it, I think you got to know the language. >> Right now for some of the students in the community, some of the kids in the community, where they don't have that grounding in their own heritage language and heritage culture, you see them creating their own culture the only way that they can know how and that would be by rejecting the mainstream culture. And so they're not going reject television and fast food, all that stuff is too fun. They're going to reject mainstream cultural institutions. And the only institution that they're really exposed to on a daily basis is the school system. And what we'd like to see more research on is the connection between lifelong satisfaction and heritage language maintenance. Does having your heritage language center you and settle you so that you're able to concentrate on academic success? >> You know, it's interesting that we if we look around the world there are about 7 to 8, 000 distinct languages, but something like 30 to 50 languages per year go extinct. So we're losing our linguistic diversity at a really alarming rate. And what will happen is if you imagine two large language communities living next door to each other, one of those languages is continually sort of out competing the other. So at every exchange in a local shop, someone's got to agree to speak the other language. Simply because English has such an enormous head start, one would have to bet on English being the language that's going to be the, sort of, you know, and this is a terrible, sort of, metaphor, the lingua franca for the world. >> If our language dies, it's gone forever. And a lot of people says, well, our Indian language is not the first language in the United States, English is now. And if we let English take over, then our language will die. >> It's not just about the language, it's who we are and what we believe in and how we live every moment of our lives. >> Until we get back into understanding what the language means to us, we're always going to be, kind of, lost just wandering around out there not understanding where we're going or where we came from our where our place in life is, you know, in this society even in America, even though we've always been here. [car door slams] The new Kituwah Academy is a total immersion program. We start with infants and then we go all the way up to five year olds. And then we go into elementary and we have roughly 40 students that are in kindergarten through third grade. And as that oldest cohort progresses, we add a grade each year. >> So those ones that have stayed with us and blazed the trail, they're the third graders now, so we're adding fourth grade next year, fifth grade the year after that, and they will be ready to go to middle school, wherever they may choose to go. >> Language revitalization has come about from different corners of Cherokee. There are different approaches to it and there still. The immersion program was probably the largest consolidated effort. It required significant resources, significant support for its continuation and that's a message to the community that this is important. >> I think it's fascinating to see this kind of process happening. This hasn't been done very much, what the eastern band of Cherokee Indians is doing here. What you have in the Cherokee language is this very strong feeling that the language is a central component of who Cherokee people have been and that there's an obligation on the current generations, the living generations to maintain that commitment to the past and to continue to speak the language in their homes and in their community and in their institutions. >> Well, it's not that we want to be different, we are different. And it doesn't mean that, you know, we can't all live in harmony in the States here, but, you know, again it goes back to our language and our culture and our traditions, you know? We're unique people and that's okay. The United States is about being diversified. >> Long ago in a small town called Cherokee, lived a girl named Summer and a boy they called Tsali. It says imagination is more important than knowledge. Sequoyah had that imagination, he had that vision. To me, he's an Einstein. His Cherokee name was Sequoyah. His work was destroyed by some of the community people, even his wife. He went back and started all over again and it was a great thing. It was a great invention, because it's helped us to preserve the language. >> It's a syllabary, because the characters in most cases approximate a syllable. So (speaking in Cherokee). It's very interesting from the perspective of the fact that you would have literally thousands of years, say, for the writing of European languages where you would go from writing it to printing it. And so for this language, he finished his work on the writing system in 1821, if I remember correctly. And then in 1828 you have the first printing form of it in the Cherokee Phoenix Newspaper. >> Let's see what we get here. This will be a proof. >> And in fact that is the second iteration of the writing system. The original one that Sequoyah developed was much more calligraphic. And so they converted it rather fast from this original calligraphic writing system to one which does look like Roman letters in a lot of cases. It was said to have been quickly taken and used and then the papers started printing in 1828. The first editor, Elias Boudinot, was very intent on promoting the need for the Cherokee to say in what is now the eastern United States and you see that move to the place where he eventually signed the final treaty removal and lost his life because of it. So the newspaper, sort of, is a microcosm of the pressures that you see one group people living under while they're trying to deal with the loss, essentially, of their country, their home. >> Just like this. See how it pops out? >> So, Tuesday we went to Hickory Tree Gap, it's an old, a very old cemetery. We did find a couple of tombstones that had the syllabary. >> Over at Cherokee on the Qualla Boundary, they don't use this line. They don't use this line. And then over here in Snowbird, sometimes they use it and sometimes they don't. So what's a good word with lay? And then over in Cherokee they would always say they would never say they'd always say >> It's so easy to learn the syllabary. Look at all the symbols in the world that you've memorized, like the Nike check. When you see that Nike check, you know what it stands for. You see that peace sign, it stands for peace, you know. All these symbols that you've learned, didn't even know you learned them. Well, Cherokee is the same way as far as reading and writing, you're just learning the symbol. The alphabet, you learn those, you know, it's a breeze. So that's the easy part of Cherokee. I talked to a linguist one time from Georgia, she said Cherokee is the hardest language I've ever tried to learn. I said, well, then it doesn't make me feel bad when I am struggling with my students and wondering why it's taking them so long to learn to speak. And she said, yes, it is so hard to learn. >> The Cherokee language is also completely different from any European language, so none of our students start out with that kind of head start with cognates in the language or similar structures in the language. And sometimes it's surprising to people in the community also that they have to come at their language now from an English language perspective. If they haven't grown up exposed to a lot of language in their home, then Cherokee language is just completely new and, to me, very exciting and quite a challenge, but to other people very frustrating. >> There is no word for word translation, one word could be a noun, adverb, adjective just depending on how you say it in a sentence. >> You can take a Cherokee word and you can change your form sometimes hundreds of ways and it changes the meaning of that word. If we say go get something, if you're going to get a glass of water, a liquid, it's different, "go get" changes. Liquid is built into the "go get". If you're going to go get a flexible item, like a shirt, it's flexible, the flexible is built into the word "go get". If you're going to go get a solid object, the solid form is built into "go get." Or go get a long and unbendable, like a fishing pole or a pencil. an animate object, something that's alive, a chicken, go get the cat, go get the dog, So that's the hard part of teaching learners when they're older. >> Cherokee is still a little more, like, detailed so we would say "we", but instead of saying, like, me and you and her, it could be just us two and then not that person or it could be all three of us. >> And so you have the whole sentence like, "I should go dancing," say, would be a single word in Cherokee language, and that's difficult for our students, sometimes, to understand. >> Reported past and definite past, I definitely went or I must have went. And I know you can do that in English, because I just did it, but in Cherokee it's just a small little "tst." >> It was our first language, that was the language we were taught at home and it wasn't hard to learn. So nowadays it's harder for the students to learn because it's not their first language, it's not the first thing they hear. >> It is a very difficult language to learn and thank God it's my first language. I would have I could never learn it. >> I think that we are getting to the children at the right time and that is birth on. >> These little ones is the where the turnaround is going to begin. They are going to take the language and the culture that they're learning now and pass it on to their children. >> Some people think that we just sit and talk Cherokee to the children and they think all they're learning is Cherokee. What they don't understand is that they're learning science, social studies, language arts, mathematics, spelling, you name it. They're learning all those content areas that English speaking schools are, but it is in the Cherokee language. >> One of the things I really want people to understand is that these children are bilingual, they're not monolingual. They don't only speak English. They don't only speak Cherokee. And I want people to understand that they'll be able to walk in both worlds. They'll be able to understand Cherokee. to understand English. And they'll be able to succeed in both worlds. >> It's in the like they're going home and not hearing the English language or when they go to town when they go Walmart or whenever they're hearing the English language all the time. It's just like the Cherokee language was when we were growing up. It's just, you know, flipped over. >> I'm very thankful for all my teachers. It's a great staff to work with and they work together well. And I know it's difficult for them because they are having we just can't go on the internet and find a worksheet on the lifecycle of a butterfly or whatever. You have it can be found on the internet then it has to be translated. So it is difficult. I can't imagine having to come up with a lesson plan that I needed that day. Sometimes you might need something right then, and in a regular English school you can just go print it out and it's there. >> In Cherokee there's no books out there so we're making it up as we go. So even in our immersioin schools, we have to just try to beat the clock and try to keep it with the grades, because it requires a lot of translation. >> We spend a lot of our time working with speakers to develop the curriculum and materials for the gradual presentation of the language to the students so that they always feel comfortable in the language and that they're always learning a little bit more of the language as they move along. >> These are all original books with native writers and native artists and these are just first grade readers here. You know, we didn't have words for modem and just basic plug ins, keyboard. I have to go to the speakers and I say, well, what would you say for this? And then they describe to me, well, what does it do? Like this one, it says (speaking in Cherokee), that means electric brain. >> Once you start learning the language, it branches out to all other areas, history, culture, traditions. When they're learning the language they're learning everything about the Cherokee people as well. >> When you are learning the language you learn, for example and that's a way to say where I'm from. I am from such and such place. But in the nuances of the language, what you're really saying is my fire is at such and such. My council house is there. I am a Cherokee of where this council fire is. And, you know, if you ask a person, a Cherokee person, you know, what do you call yourself as a tribal person? They would say they're Cherokee, maybe, but Cherokee is not a Cherokee word, it has no meaning it's something that was applied, a name that was applied to the Cherokee people. You would hear which means the real people or the principal people. Or you might hear So you're literally saying I am a person of Kituwah, of this place. >> My grandpa used to say to me, like this, and he would maybe have a knife, you know, and pull it away from me. And I thought he was saying for the longest time, "Give it to me." So I thought he was teasing me. And I took it as And then later on I found out that it meant lend me this. He was saying, "Lend me this," you know, and it was much more polite. >> There's words in Cherokee that are just so specific. The word really can't be translated into English because there's so many things it encompasses, but it's one of the cornerstones of our program. And the closest translation would be the right path, the right way, but there's so many components that that encompasses that we can't just give them a word in English. >> (Speaking in Cherokee) that's a powerful word, it's hard to explain. Some people say it just means straight, but to me it means so much more. It means it's a way of being in the right way. It carries there I can't even think of how to put that into words. >> Within the language are values and traditions and history and ways of life. That's what speaking Cherokee is about. And so without that, we lose all of that. We don't just lose something that's very difficult or something that's, oh, our elderly people speak Cherokee, we lose who really the heart and soul of who we are as Cherokee people. >> This is what is so critical and so important to our identity and our future as a people is to continue on making this same sound that was given to us by the creator, our language. It's a sound that hasn't been heard here for 60 years, the sound of children speaking our language. >> If we go some places and we start seeing our friends or something, we usually, like, go up and speak Cherokee sometimes. Then when you look around people are just like, wow. >> My son, we actually ran into his uncle at Family Dollar, I mean just a store, and him and his wife was talking in the language. They were talking about a goose, but they were referring to it as a duck and it was on the top shelf. And the uncle was sitting there calling it a And here Jon* was, he was probably about three or four at the time, and he's like And the uncle turned around and he looked, he looked back at his wife, and he looked back at Jon and he's like, do you speak Cherokee? But he was talking to him in Cherokee this whole time. And he's like uh huh. And his wife is standing back and her eyes teared up and my eyes teared up. It's just, my son can have a conversation with two of our oldest fluent speakers and know what they're talking about and respond in the language. And seeing not only him but other kids speaking our native language, it's amazing. It just touches my heart. >> I like Cherokee because it's very rare and a lot of people think that it's just a language, but, like, if you're Cherokee it's like your home. >> This total immersion program is not a school, it's a home. And so from the very foundation and the design and everything that we've put into it is that we are not peers, your classmates are not classmates, we are family. Your classmates are not just your friends, they're equivalent to cousins and brothers and sisters. It wears and tugs on your heart every single day. There are moments of joy when you walk into the classroom, when you hear the babies speaking and understanding the language, and you're running on the playground and they're speaking Cherokee, and walking into the cafeteria and they are playing rock, paper, scissors, but they're doing it in Cherokee. And you sit down with a pencil and paper you're trying to figure out how you can keep this going, and it's rough work. It's hard. So you have to surround yourself by dedicated people who feel the same way about the language as you do. We can look at the newspaper every week as it comes out and we circle the names of the people that we lost that are fluent speakers and we look at how we're going to be able to survive in the next five years so that this program and this language doesn't die, because if we shut our doors the language will just become a list of vocabulary words that are taught from dictionaries. >> They're hearing the language all the time and they're reading and writing it, but when they leave here it's hard for them to continue to do that because their parents are not bilingual. It makes it hard for the child to continue. >> The thing is it's almost we're running out of time, because we're losing too many speakers. >> Thinking about the number of native speakers who are fluent, it's a rather sobering circumstance to think about a language in that situation. There are many languages around the world that are facing the same circumstance. some people say, well, it's your generation Lou, after you're gone it's not going to be alive, but I think I'm making a harder effort knowing that if it's going to die with me then I need to do something to preserve it and so I keep talking to these kids. And some of the high school kids that's been in Shirley's class, if I drive to McDonald's there's a boy there and I start talking and he'll think about it. And he says he has to think about it, but some of the words come back. And so I think that's how you preserve your language. >> Keep talking to your kids, your grandkids and that's the only way we're going to get it done. You have the have the whole community support, the majority of the people realizing, you know, how important it is. >> I have a hard sometimes because I live in this world, but one thing that I know is I live on this Indian land and I have all the benefits of being a Cherokee, not just because I live here, but because that blood flows through me. I have a responsibility to walk both worlds. If I don't have my culture to ground me, then I really don't belong here. >> There has to be an understanding and a value and the community has to embrace the idea that Cherokee language it what makes us Cherokee, that having an enrollment card doesn't make you Cherokee it just makes you a number within a government within a government. >> And you'll hear that in this work quite a bit, oh, you can't be this kind of person unless you speak this language. And the problem with that is languages can be so difficult to hang on to that you're saying that you're own children, even, are not your own people. And so I think that rhetoric is a little bit too harsh, but definitely that would be one of the major drives for learning the Cherokee language is your, kind of, sense of Cherokee heritage and the language as who you are or who you want to be. I'd like to think about it a little bit more aspirationally. So learning now would be to say that you're a part of the Cherokee community and it's a beautiful community. If we consider what it actually means to be a pluralistic society, then that means we're going to have to make space for people who speak different languages, who think different ways, who have different cultures inside of a national culture or a global culture. And so all the movement has been in the opposite direction towards globalization, towards homogenization, you know. What does it mean to change the process and open up space for a plurality of different small cultures working together? How can we truly accept and respect those people and allow them some measure of autonomy with their educational system and the language that they speak? >> Yesterday I was walking around, it was children's day, and I saw so many young people have these tattoos and they have the syllabary writing on them and I was amazed. I feel proud that they are willing to celebrate their nativeness, their Cherokee self with that, but I would hope that-- I don't want people just to put syllabarian on their bodies, I want syllabary, not syllabary, I want the Cherokee language to come out of their mouths. I want them to learn how to speak. I want them to celebrate our culture by keeping our language alive. My girls go to New Kituwah. I know that they're learning their language and they're learning their culture and, you know, I'm staking their futures that they are going to be okay and that they're going to be okay in the culture and that the culture is going to take care of them and they're going to be educated there. And so I don't know where it's going to go, but I think we're moving in a good direction. >> I resent the word now when I hear someone say it's dying or it's a dead language, because it isn't, it's very much still out there. I think that we can turn that around and really turn it around by saying we're speaking it. The children are speaking it. It's alive. >> Everyone wants to see how our children are going to balance two languages and still be successful in education with the English speaking world. There's a lot on these kid's shoulders that a lot of people don't really realize. >> In English, that's not my language. I speak it, but it's not mine. If I speak in Cherokee, it's coming from the heart, because that's mine. That's my language. 00:54:59.296,00:00:00.000 [chanting]

- Everything You Need to Know About Snowbird Retirement | Ep. 12: Kind: captions Language: en For a lot of Canadian retirees, there is a dream that comes with that retirement. It's sun, it's palm trees, it's the snowbird lifestyle where you spend part of the winter somewhere warm, [music] then you return home for the summer. Now, that all sounds great, but once you dig in, there's more to it than just packing your suitcase [music] and booking a flight. Welcome to episode 12 of Second Act. This is the series that we've designed to help Canadians navigate retirement with clarity and confidence. [music] Now, in the video today, I'm going to cover off the key financial and legal planning decisions behind the snowbird [music] dream. I'm going to look at why so many Canadians become snowbirds, the daycount and tax rules that you must follow, healthcare and travel insurance risks, the true cost of this lifestyle, renting versus owning a place down south, and crossber legal and [music] estate concerns. Thank you to Harvest ETS for supporting this entire series. I will be speaking more about them a little bit later in the video. Let's start with the obvious question. Why do so many Canadians choose the snowbird lifestyle? No doubt big part of that is escaping winter. You know, we've spent decades scraping windshields. The appeal of sunshine is pretty obvious, but there is more to the snowbird lifestyle than just warmer weather. Cold weather actually takes a toll on our aging bodies. Our joints, our energy levels, maybe even our mood in some cases, the shorter days, the longer nights, they can start to wear on you. Even simple errands start to feel like a bit of a chore. And then you retire and for the first time you control your own schedule. You don't have to ask anybody for vacation. You don't have to rush home on a Sunday night to be at work on Monday morning. You can finally ask yourself, if I could spend my time anywhere, where would I go? That is the true appeal of the snowbird lifestyle. It's the choice to live differently. Now, in this stage of life, time is your most valuable asset and where you spend it can make all the difference. [music] Let's look at that point where the snowbird dream bumps into legal reality. Because as tempting as it is to extend your stay down south, there is a hard line that you cannot afford to cross. Now, you've probably heard of the 183day rule. If you spend more than 183 days in the US in a calendar year, the IRS could consider you to be a US tax resident, even if you are a full-time Canadian citizen. That can open the door to things like US income tax filing obligations, possible double taxation on certain types of income, exposure to US estate tax even on your Canadian assets, and conflicts with your existing Canadian legal and financial structures. But here is where so many snowbirds get caught off guard. There is also what's called the substantial presence test. And this is a formula the IRS uses to total up your time in the US over 3 years. So it adds all of the days from the current year, onethird of the days from the previous year, then one sixth of the days from 2 years ago. And if that total hits 183 days or more, you could be deemed to be a US resident for tax purposes. And that is why tracking your days matters so much. You have to track them precisely. You can't round them. You can't estimate them. If you overstay by just a few days or a few weeks, even if it's due to illness, flight delays, you could face major tax consequences. If you're at all unsure, get professional advice. Talk with a qualified crossborder tax adviser who can help you calculate your day count, evaluate your risk, and walk you through options if needed. The US tax system is complex and when it comes to your residency status, it's always better to ask early than try and fix it later. Here's another area where a lot of first-time snowbirds get caught off guard. Every Canadian province has residency requirements for their public health insurance. Now, in most cases, you're going to need to be physically present in your home province for at least five to six months per calendar year in order to maintain your coverage. Now, this doesn't just mean having an address there. It means actually being there, and you're going to need your documentation to back it up if they ask for that. If you spend too long outside the country, your provincial coverage that could lapse, and getting it reinstated isn't always an automatic exercise. This is an obvious issue that creates real exposure, especially if you're dealing with chronic health conditions, prescriptions, or if you need care on short-term notice. Then there's the US health care system, which isn't cheap, and I'm putting that mildly. Even a short visit to an emergency room can cost thousands of dollars. A broken leg, a mild stroke, that could quickly run into five or six figures. It is the kind of thing that can derail a retirement plan literally overnight. So, obviously, medical travel insurance is non-negotiable, but this is what a lot of people miss. Not all policies are created equal. Before you buy, you're going to want to review coverage limits. Does the policy cover a million dollars, 5 million, or more exclusions, especially for high-risk activities or destinations. Pre-existing conditions. Now, some policies won't cover them or only after a short stability period. Deductibles. Know what you're going to have to pay out of pocket. and trip length factors in here. Many policies cap your coverage at 30 days, 60 days, 90 days and after that you may have to return home or reapply. Also consider emergency scenarios. If you are hospitalized, will you be treated there or will you be stabilized and shipped back to Canada? And here is a critical detail. If your health changes between now and your departure, a new prescription, a flare up, even just a test in many cases, you're going to have to update your insurer, or you may risk having them deny a claim. In just a few minutes, I'm going to look at the numerous costs that snowbirds need to be aware of. And one of those costs, of course, is travel. You've probably noticed travel isn't getting any cheaper. And when the prices rise, but planes, hotels, cruise ships are still packed, that tells you something about demand. And that's exactly what the Harvest Travel and Leisure Index ETF, ticker TVL, is designed to capture. This fund gives you exposure to a portfolio of global travel leaders, airlines, hotel chains, cruise lines, booking platforms, and entertainment companies. And these are household names that benefit directly from the rising travel activity and strong consumer demand that we're seeing these days. The fund is built around a focused index of leading travel and insurance companies. And for investors who want to generate more cash flow from the same group of companies, Harvest also offers TRVI, the Harvest Travel and Leisure Income ETF. It holds the same underlying portfolios as TRVL, but it uses an active covered call strategy to generate additional cash flow, which could be especially useful if you're drawing income in retirement. So whether you want exposure to travel sector growth or a higher income version of the same strategy, you can learn more about both of these funds at harvestf.com. Just scan the QR codes on your screen or click in the link in the description. Being a snowbird can absolutely be affordable, but we do have to keep it real. If you do not go into this venture with clear eyes and a well-built budget, then the lifestyle that was meant to bring you the freedom can quietly create a lot of financial strain. Let's look now at a few costs that tend to be consistently underestimated, especially by first- timers. Starting with travel, medical insurance. We covered the risks in detail just a few minutes ago, so I'm not going to repeat them here. But from a budgeting standpoint, this is one of the largest and most underestimated snowbird expenses. So just make sure that insurance is clearly built into your own snowbird budget. Secondly, currency exchange and fees. Every time you spend euros or US dollars using a Canadian credit card or convert money, there's a bike taken out by fees, exchange rates, or both. Now, over a few months, these small hits can add up. Think about it as well. If the looney dips, your spending power drops, too. So you should have a strong budgeting strategy in place that accounts for currency fluctuation risk. A lot of snowbirds set up a dedicated US dollar account to deal with this issue. Also consider transportation and travel logistics. Think of flights, rental cars, airport parking, extra luggage fees. There are a lot of little costs in between just getting there and back. And if you're a snowbird who likes to return home for the holidays, family events, medical appointments, then those repeat trips can quickly eat into your annual travel budget. Also, property related expenses. If you own a second home at your destination of choice, you have to think beyond just the purchase price. You will be responsible for property taxes, which are often higher for non-residents, HOA or park fees, maintenance and repairs, utilities and insurance, including special coverage for vacant periods. Even if you are renting, peak season prices can vary wildly by location and year, especially in higher demand areas like Florida or Arizona. Now, here's one that catches a lot of people offguard. Vacation mode spending, I'm going to call it. When you are in a sunny environment, meeting friends, going out more, sightseeing, spending can ramp up pretty quickly and you might not even notice it. You might eat out more, you might shop differently or book excursions that you normally wouldn't splurge on back at home. There's nothing wrong with that, but it does need to be part of your plan so that it does not get out of control. Bottom line here, you don't need to be wealthy to be a snowbird, but you do need a wellthoughtout, sustainable plan. The goal is to make sure that the lifestyle that you've dreamed of fits comfortably within your retirement and so that the pros always outweigh the cons. One of the biggest questions that regular snowbirds face is whether to rent a place each year or buy a second property. The truth here is that there is no one-sizefits-all answer. I want to start with the case for owning. Now, some retirees love having a permanent home base down south. Owning provides familiarity. You get to know your neighbors, your community, your routine. Consistency. No scrambling for a rental or wondering if prices will spike next year. Personalization. you can actually make your space truly yours. There's also potential savings over the long run, especially if prices rise. But owning also means you have tied up capital in a non-liquid asset, ongoing maintenance, taxes, insurance, and HOA fees, estate and crossber tax implications, especially if it's a US property, and a need to coordinate property management while you're away. So, if you are committed to a snowbird lifestyle and want to set roots in one community, owning can work. Just make sure that it aligns with your overall financial and estate plan. Now, I do want to look at the flip side. Renting does offer freedom, flexibility, especially in the early years. This is ideal if you just want to try out the lifestyle. If you want to test different locations before settling into one, if you'd rather avoid the responsibilities of home ownership, your health, your finances, family needs, they may change from year to year. One drawback is that rental places can fluctuate, and in peak months, demand is often very high, but renting does give you options. You aren't locked into a particular location or a community if it turns out that it's not the right fit for you. If you are new to this and still figuring out what works, it's probably best to start by renting. Think of it sort of as an extended factf finding trip. You're going to learn what size of home suits your needs, whether you prefer gated communities, RV resorts, beach condos, how accessible local healthc care, transportation, and amenities are, and how the lifestyle actually feels once the novelty wears off. After a season or two, you're going to be in a far better position to decide whether owning makes sense or whether the flexibility is still the right choice for you. Legal and estate planning is often a part of the snowbird list [music] that gets overlooked until something goes wrong. But if you own a property in the US or if you spend a significant amount of time there, then crossborder legal planning is essential. owning US property, even just a modest condo or a trailer, that could expose you to US estate tax. And this can apply even if you're a Canadian citizen and resident. The threshold for US estate tax is much lower for non-citizens than it is for Americans. And it is based on the value of your US situated assets at death. It doesn't matter if your overall estate is under the Canadian threshold. If the IRS sees you as having a taxable US estate, your heirs might be in for a nasty surprise. Now we should also talk about crossber wills and powers of attorney. Another important thing to consider here is that the standard Canadian will and power of attorney might not be recognized or enforceable in certain US jurisdictions. You may need a crossber will or at a minimum a US compliant will that addresses your US assets. Powers of attorney that meet legal standards in both countries for property and for healthcare. updated advanced medical directives that travel with you, especially if you're spending months at a time away. If you were incapacitated while you're abroad, your family will need documents that local hospitals and financial institutions actually recognize. Now, you also want to simplify things for your loved ones. Consider preparing an estate summary sheet. This is a single document that lists where your key assets are located, who your legal contacts are, what your wishes are if something happens, and how to access digital records and passwords. This doesn't replace legal documents, but it can help prevent chaos if your family suddenly does need to step in. Very, very important here. This is not DIY territory. The rules are complex. The consequences of doing something wrong or nothing is very expensive and can be stressful for your family. Again here, a good crossber estate lawyer can help ensure that your documents and your intentions hold up on both sides of the border. I hope by this point in the episode it is clear that snowboarding is a lifestyle choice and the people who thrive in it are the ones who actually treat it that way. They build in a rhythm between the two homes, two communities and two ways of life. They stay rooted in Canada. They maintain close ties here with your family, your friends, and the causes that you care about. At the same time, they do create meaningful routines and relationships wherever they land during the winter months. That could mean attending a local church or community center, volunteering part-time while you're abroad, staying active with things like golf, yoga, walking classes, dance classes, pickle ball, hosting friends or family for part of the season, or even continuing part-time remote work or projects that you are passionate about. There is something that these retirees all have in common and that is intentionality. They know that this is not a vacation. It's sort of a chapter of retirement that they are writing on their own terms. As I talked about way back in episode one of this series, retirement is a lot more than just not [music] working. It is about choosing how you want to live. And for a lot of Canadians, that snowbird life is a beautiful way to make the most of this chapter. This episode wraps up our second act series. 12 episodes that were designed to help Canadians transition into your own retirement with clarity, with confidence. And if these videos helped you, consider sharing them with someone that you know who's getting ready for their own second act. As always, thank you so much for watching. I'll see you in another episode in the series. I'll put [music] the playlist right here.

- What Rules Apply To State Income Taxes For Snowbird Retirees? - Budgeting as a Retiree: Kind: captions Language: en What rules apply to state income taxes for snowbird retirees? Imagine spending your winters in a warm, sunny place while keeping your home in a cooler state. Many retirees do this, but it can get tricky with state income taxes. Let's break down what you need to know to stay on top of your tax game. Most states decide if you're a resident based on where your main home is and how many days you spend there. If you spend more than 183 days in a state, you are usually considered a resident for tax purposes. That means you pay income taxes on all your income, no matter where it comes from. So tracking your days in each state is really important to avoid surprises. If you earn income in both your home state and your winter state, you might face taxes from both. Some states tax based on where you live, others on where the income is earned. Many states offer credits for taxes paid to another state to prevent double taxation. But rules vary, so it's good to understand how each state treats pensions, social security, and investment income. Nexus is another key point. It means a state has enough connection to tax you. If you own property, have a business, or spend enough time there, that state might tax your income even if you are not a full-time resident. This can happen if you own a second home or do business in that state. Some states are more retirey friendly. Florida and Texas do not have state income taxes, making them popular choices for snowbirds. On the other hand, states like Arizona and California do tax retirement income with different rates and rules. Washington state has no income tax, but recently added a capital gains tax on large transactions, which can affect retirees with big investment gains. To establish residency in your winter state, you should claim homestead exemptions, get a driver's license there, register to vote, and spend more than half the year in that state. Continuing to claim residency benefits in your original state can cause it to consider you a resident, leading to unexpected taxes. If you are taking required minimum distributions from retirement accounts, plan carefully. Some states tax these distributions, others do not. Timing your withdrawals and understanding each state's rules can help you keep your tax bill low. For budgeting, keep detailed records of your days in each state. Know how each state taxes different types of income. Consider living in states with no income tax during winter months. Establish clear residency in your snowbird state by taking legal steps. And always consult a tax professional who understands multi-state rules. By understanding these rules and planning ahead, you can enjoy your retirement years without worrying about unexpected tax bills. Staying informed helps you make smart choices so your money lasts longer and your future stays secure.

I have&nbsp;seen snowbirds buy beautiful properties and then&nbsp;spend every winter dealing with snow removal and&nbsp;maintenance issues and insurance concerns, and&nbsp;just constant phone calls from afar. So today,&nbsp;I'm going to break down the best home types for&nbsp;snowbirds in Vernon, and just as importantly,&nbsp;the types of homes that look great but usually&nbsp;cause the most stress. I'm Lisa Salt with RE/MAX&nbsp;Vernon Salt Fowler. This is Video #3 in my Snowbird Living in Vernon,&nbsp;BC series — where we talk honestly about how to&nbsp;make seasonal living easy and not complicated. If you haven't watched the first two videos&nbsp;yet, I'll link the playlist below, but this&nbsp;one is all about what kind of home actually&nbsp;works when you're gone for months at a time. Now, what do snowbirds really need from a home? And I mean, before we talk about the specific&nbsp;home types, let's clarify the goal. Most snowbirds&nbsp;want to lock the door and leave. They want minimal&nbsp;winter maintenance. No emergency phone calls ideally, and insurance that's easy to manage, and the peace of mind while they're away. So&nbsp;that means snowbird-friendly homes need exterior maintenance included, snow removal handled, clear&nbsp;strata or property management, and neighbours nearby year-round. Now, with that in mind, let's&nbsp;start with the best options for all those things. #1 is townhomes. This is the&nbsp;snowbird sweet spot. They're often the&nbsp;best overall choice for snowbirds in Vernon. And why do they work so well? Well, because&nbsp;of all those things I mentioned earlier: Exterior&nbsp;maintenance is included, snow removal is handled,&nbsp;private garage or parking, more space than&nbsp;a condo. So that's a consideration. When&nbsp;you come back, you want to have enough space&nbsp;to live, and easier insurance for seasonal absences. Townhomes tend to offer the perfect&nbsp;balance. You still feel like you have a home, but without the full responsibility of a detached&nbsp;house. They're especially popular in places like&nbsp;Predator Ridge, The Seasons that we already&nbsp;talked about, Okanagan Landing developments. #2 is condos. And condos are the best&nbsp;for maximum simplicity. If you want maximum&nbsp;lock-and-leave living, condos are hard to beat. They're ideal for snowbirds who travel very often,&nbsp;want minimal responsibility, prefer&nbsp;elevators and single-level living,&nbsp;or can handle elevators and single-level living,&nbsp;and don't want to think about maintenance at&nbsp;all. The pros are the building maintenance&nbsp;things are handled, snow removal is covered,&nbsp;on-site management is super easy, and&nbsp;there's security monitoring. The cons,&nbsp;though: strata rules vary, might not be something&nbsp;that you want. Storage can be limited. The key&nbsp;here is to choose a well-run building with&nbsp;clear strata policies for extended absences. Now, #3, let's talk about bare land&nbsp;strata properties. That's another excellent&nbsp;snowbird option, a very popular one, and they work&nbsp;well because you own your own home and the land,&nbsp;but there's not a lot of headaches. Often&nbsp;landscaping and snow removal are included,&nbsp;and often you get ground-level living&nbsp;as well. Often located in quiet,&nbsp;adult-oriented communities, maybe gated, and they're really popular with retirees, snowbirds especially, who want the one-level&nbsp;living and buyers downsizing from larger homes. The other option is a detached home, but proceed&nbsp;with caution. They aren't automatically wrong&nbsp;for snowbirds, but they do require planning. Challenges include things like snow removal. You’ve got to figure that out. Winter property&nbsp;checks, insurance restrictions. Normally,&nbsp;you have to have someone come and check&nbsp;once every few days. What about emergency&nbsp;response while you're away? Detached homes&nbsp;only work well for snowbirds if they're in&nbsp;a managed or gated community and you have&nbsp;reliable local support and the maintenance&nbsp;is simplified. Otherwise, they can quickly&nbsp;become a pain in the butt. Very stressful. Now, what home types should snowbirds avoid? These are the homes that cause the most problems: large detached homes with no strata, acreages&nbsp;or rural properties, especially if you have&nbsp;animals. What do you do then? Long private&nbsp;driveways, potentially steep ones. You have&nbsp;to keep those clear, maybe for the mail carrier or&nbsp;somebody coming to the door, they slip and fall,&nbsp;and all of a sudden you get sued. That's a drag. And properties with frequent winter issues. They may be beautiful, but they're not designed&nbsp;for lock-and-leave living. They just aren't. Now, quick pause.
