- Trump’s Trade War Sends Canadian Snowbirds Packing ||  A Billion-Dollar Winter Exodus: Kind: captions Language: en The annual migration of hundreds of thousands of Canadian snowbirds escaping freezing temperatures in their homeland and heading to warmer US states such as Florida for the duration of the winter could be about to become noticeably thinner. Many have ditched plans to visit their southern neighbor and are looking to spend their valuable dollars elsewhere. largely put off by Donald Trump's escalating economic war with Canada and strict new immigration rules that have created fear and confusion, there's some resistance. There's always someone calls in from Canada and says, "No, no more us. Before we were friends [music] and now enemies," said Richard Clevat, a Canadian originally from Quebec who's owned a motel and apartment rental business in [music] Fort Lauderdale. Popular with snowbirds for more than three decades. Bookings have declined. Our rates are a little lower to try to attract [music] them. In March and April, a lot of people kind of walked away from the deposits, which wasn't so bad because we were able to rent the rooms to others. But in the summer, what was bad was they also didn't book [music] for the following year, and that hurt us financially. The boycott, which began last winter after Trump's election to a second term as president, an imposition of trade tariffs appears to be gathering pace. According to research by the Travel Health Insurance Association of Canada, it promises to put a severe dent in the $20.5 billion snowbirds traditionally pump into the US economy between late October and April. The study found only 26% of Canadians intend to take a US vacation this winter, down from 41%. Last year, and of those 61 and older, only 1 in 10 plan to visit, compared to one in three a year ago. Canada's own tourism industry, meanwhile, [music] is reporting record revenue. Buoied by visitors who decided to stay home, the sector took [music] in 59 billion Canadians, 42 billion from May to August, a 6% increase on 2014. American visitors to Canada dropped 1.7% during [music] that same period. Business owners such as Clavit and realtors in popular snowbird states such as Arizona, Florida, Texas, and Hawaii are reporting significantly fewer rental bookings and higher than usual numbers of Canadians seeking to offload second houses and condos. More than half of Canadians with homes in the US, 54% are considering selling in the next 12 months, with 62% of those citing the political situation as their main reason, according to research published in August. While some blame a weak Canadian dollar and rising travel costs for their decision not to travel, 40% also site political [music] tensions with the US. Trump has frequently assailed Canada and its political [music] leaders, recently retaliating for an anti-tariff advertisement posted by the Ontario government by slapping an additional 10% tariff on imports from a country he has repeatedly taunted as the 51st state. of equal concern to traveling Canadians, experts say are a raft [music] of invasive new immigration measures imposed by the Trump administration have left many uncomfortable about crossing the border. From December, all non-American visitors will be photographed on both their arrival and departure from the US. The move follows new enforcement this year of an obligation on Canadians visiting for longer than 30 days to register their presence and whereabouts with the US government. Ryan Ratchkovski, director of research and communications at the Toronto-based Canadian Snowbird Association, said there was confusion over the registration policy, which he said was being enforced erratically at points of immigration. Some members, [music] he said, had been subjected to secondary screening featuring the collection of biometric data, including fingerprints and photographs, while others experienced smooth entries. There is so much inconsistency right now. It's based on the border officer that you get, based on the port of entry that you're entering the US from. And because of that, we're reproviding our members with a warning and letting them know that this might be a possibility. He said our message to snowbirds every year is be prepared. This year, obviously, people are going to be prepared in a different way. Rajkovsky said he believed the drop in travel by Canadians would be more by shorter term vacationers than the estimated. 900,000 snowbirds who traditionally spend up to the permitted 6 months in the US but acknowledged many were uneasy about the political situation. The longerterm travelers, some of those individuals have made it clear to us they don't want to travel to the US [music] this coming season, but I would say the vast majority, particularly those that own homes in the US, will be making the trip this year as they always have. He said, "Economic and political headwinds are making the snowbird lifestyle more difficult," he added. In Florida, prices are going up, particularly for things like insurance coverage, and the Canadian dollar isn't [music] helping as well. So, it's a much more complex picture than just looking at it from a political standpoint. Analysts say any significant drop in snowbird visits could be catastrophic for states where they are among the biggest spenders during the winter months. The snowbird economy brings in an estimated $20.5 billion annually in direct spending, property, and [music] sales taxes and supports millions of jobs, especially in tourism, hospitality, and retail. The US Travel Association warned earlier this year that even a 10% drop in travel would mean 2 million fewer visits and cost 2.1 billion in loss spending with local businesses [music] suffering the most. You've got businesses that have to recalibrate their projections regarding the number of visitors, which has a very significant ripple effect throughout the manufacturing sector and supply chains all the way into the travel and tourism industries, but also much beyond that, said Valerie Krooks of [music] Simon Fraser University and an expert in snowbird demographics and behaviors. There are entire hospitals that bring in seasonal workers to address the demand by the influx of snowbirds from the northern US and Canada. And when you change the numbers, you change everything about how a particular hospital system is able to operate. Crooks said the more obstacles that are placed in the path of snowbirds, the more likely they are to take themselves and their money elsewhere, such as Mexico. The second most popular destination for Canadian winter travelers. One of the things that being a snowbird is really predicated on is the ease of movement across the border. This is the next step of sentiment [music] that people are concerned about that political will can seemingly change on a whim. An ease of access across the border shifts because of a change brought about by a government administration. She said there are countries all over the world looking to attract people to come and stay for winter seasons and they regoing to look even more attractive when an established destination like the southern part of the US is no longer somewhere. Americans and many Canadians feel comfortable going. Cleette the Fort Lauderdale rental business owner disagrees. As it gets cold and those real snowbirds starts to fly this way, others will follow. - More Canadian snowbirds decide wintering in the U.S. over economic tensions: Kind: captions Language: en It seems more Canadian snowbirds are deciding to fly elsewhere this winter to avoid the US as their vacation destination. >> number of Canadians crossing the border has been steadily dropping and a new survey finds the trend is likely to continue well into next year. Here's Pat Foran and Consumer Alert, Pat. Well, thanks Nathan and Michelle. Next month many retired Canadians would normally be packing their suitcases to head to Florida, Arizona and other sunny US destinations, but a new survey shows a massive drop in snowbirds planning to cross the border choosing other hotspots instead. More Canadians are turning their backs on travel to the US deciding to either stay home or choose sunny destinations somewhere else. A survey by the Travel Health Insurance Association of Canada found that only 26% are likely to go to the US. That's a 37% drop from last year. The survey found of those 61 years of age and older only 10% are likely to go to the US. That's a 66% drop from the year before. We've certainly seen a move by some to get away from maybe ownership or long-term rentals in the US in favor of other southern climates. The survey found Canadians are concerned about rising travel costs, political tensions, exchange rates and safety. The shift comes at a time when the US is introducing new entry requirements which mean Canadians staying for longer than 30 days need to register and provide their fingerprints, be photographed and pay a $30 fee. Having your photo taken and your your fingerprints done feels really weird and wrong to us, but the reality is is many countries all over the world have already been doing this for the last decade. Travel expert Barry Choi says while travel to the US is down, he believes many families will continue to go to US attractions, but keep a lower profile and not post on social media. He says snowbirds who don't own property in the US will also find it hard to stay away. If someone's a snowbird and they own a vacation home in in Florida or Arizona, of course they're going to go. Canadians avoiding the states are also planning to spend winter months in Europe including Portugal and Spain. Trips to Mexico and the Caribbean will also depend on the weather and the cost of travel. If American destinations cut prices, it may be enough for some to reconsider a US trip. If some of the US destinations start to go on sale, maybe that easy trip over the border still prevails. And wherever you go, if you leave Canada, you're advised to get travel insurance in case of a medical emergency or unexpected health issue. When you're out of the country, both are not covered under Canadian health plans. On your side, I'm Pat Foran. If you have a consumer story idea, email us at alert@ctv.ca.

- Medicare for Snowbirds  (and RV travel): Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I am Matthew Claassen with MedigapSeminars.org in this video we're gonna go over all you need to know about Medicare for Snowbirds, for RV'ers, as well as for people who are on Medicare and expecting to move to a different state. MedigapSeminars.org; An Independent Insurance Broker Specializing in Medicare. Are you a Snowbird; a person who has homes in more than one state and tends to move south to a warmer climate in the winter? Or perhaps you're in RV'er in a motor home and like to travel all over the country. Or you're simply a person on Medicare and expect to move. You want to know how that's going to impact your Medicare what decisions you have to make. If so, then you should have questions about how being a Snowbird or an RV'er or moving impacts your Medicare. What kind of decisions you need to make and what your Medicare rights are. That is what we will discuss in this video, and we are going to approach it sort of a frequently asked questions (FAQ) format. We handle Medicare questions and we handle Medicare for people literally coast-to-coast, from California to Virginian, and from Michigan to Miami. We are based in South Florida and because of that we tend to get a lot of questions from Florida Snowbirds or people moving down south, and it has become an area of expertise. The first question we tend to get, a fairly simple one; "what is the right Medicare plan for a person who's in multiple states during the year?" The answer to that question isn't so much what is the right plan as it is what is the wrong plan. When you sign up for Medicare, once you've enrolled, you get a red white and blue card that shows you have Medicare ... Medicare Part A and Medicare Part B. That is called "Original Medicare". That original Medicare, along with any supplement that you get with that, is good in every state. You can go to any doctor, any medical facility as long as they accept Medicare, anywhere in the country. You don't need to have a Physician give you permission to see a specialist. You have complete control over your Medicare. The other route you can take is enrolling in a Medicare Advantage plan. Medicare Advantage plans are typically HMO's and PPO's. There are some other types, but most are HMO's and PPO's. That's local coverage! That tends to be just you county, or just the counties surrounding your area year. If you've chosen an HMO, then you have no coverage, outside of emergency services, outside of your network. None whatsoever. You've lost your right to have insurance outside of your network. If you have a PPO, then you will have coverage outside of your network, but it will be at a higher cost. With the PPO you can see a doctor either in network, or out of network. In network, which tends to be your local area, you will have copays and deductibles and a maximum annual out of pocket expense of no more than $6700 a year (for 2017). If you go out of network it's gonna cost you more. It will cost you higher copays or higher deductibles, and your maximum annual out-of-pocket can be as high as $10,000 per year. There are regional PPO's that can offer some extended in-network coverage out-of-state, but that's beyond the scope of this particular video. The bottom line is that if you are a Snowbird or you're in RV'er, you want to avoid the Medicare Advantage plans. They don't typically satisfy your needs because you have multiple states with which you might want to have health insurance coverage. They will come back and say; but we have emergency services outside of our network anywhere in the country. Very true, but you have to read the fine print on an emergency service. Often times an emergency service is defined as simply stabilizing you until your life is no longer threatened. A perfect example is someone's traveling out of state and gets in an automobile accident and is in traction with almost everything broken. Emergency service is making sure that you're alive and stable. After that you have to get yourself back to your service area for coverage. You have to get back in-network. Otherwise it is out-of-network coverage and that can be very, very expensive. If you're a Snowbird or RV'er you would want to avoid Medicare Advantage plans. If however you find that the Medicare supplements are simply too expensive for you, or the average Medicare supplement, then you might want to consider something called Medicare Supplement Plan F - high deductible. Or, as will be introduced in 2020, the Plan G- high deductible. These are not as high of coverage as you would get with the Plan F or Plan G or Plan N or any of the other more popular supplements, but you still have the freedom to go to any doctor in any state and any medical facility that accepts Medicare. Your cost is typically under $100 a month and, depending on where you live, it could actually be as low as $50.00 a month or even lower, I should say. So, you can check on that. We actually have an entire video on Medicare Plan F - high deductible. You might want to look at it if you're in that situation. But I would only suggest it if your finances really dictate that decision. The next most common question we get is; Which state do I use for as resident? I spent eight months in Pennsylvania and only four months in Florida. Does that make Pennsylvania my residence state? Or I spend six months and one and six months in the other. Well, the answer is that on all of these contracts, on all insurance contracts it says you put your permanent address. The definition of "permanent address" is actually written into the Social Security law. That's where Medicare laws are written, in with Social Security. Your "permanent address" is where you vote and pay taxes. It should also be the same address you have on record at Social Security. It doesn't matter if you spend only two months in Florida. If Florida it is your permanent address, that's where you have to buy your insurance policies from. This includes your prescription drug Part D, your supplement, or anything else Medicare related. It doesn't matter how much time. It matters where you vote and where you pay your taxes. Of course we also get then; what if I, you know, cheat a little? Well, this a contract. On a contract you have to write down the information and sign your name. If you're untruthful on a contract, then like any other contract, it could be voided. Insurance companies are not going to check your residence state when you sign up, apply and get accepted. But they have written into every single one of these policies that they have the right to check if something happens. So if you have a medical event, you're in the hospital the insurance company is looking at hundreds of thousands of dollars in costs, they have the right to check to make sure that everything is true on your application. If they find it wasn't truthful, they have the right to void the contract. That is a decision that can be made, and of course it would be made when you are an absolute worst circumstances. I look at insurance is it's, it's... what we use to mitigate financial risks. Well if you're not truthful on the application then you're not mitigating financial risk. You're just changing it from one scenario to another. That's not a wise decision. The next question would be; what if you have Medicare and you decide to move? You decide to change your permanent resident address. What happens? Well, first off if you have a Medicare supplement, that is good in every single state. It's good every where you go. You do not need to do anything. If you have a Medicare supplement and there's the exact same supplement, offer by the exact same company in a different state then ... that company has the right to raise your price if it's a higher price in that state. You have the right to ask for a lower price if the supplement is a lower price in that state. The prices in every state are different. If you have questions on that, contact us will be able to give you some more specific information. But keep in mind a lot of these companies have one subsidiary working in one state another subsidiary working in another. Those are not the same policies. policies. If they don't have the same policy, they can't do anything with the price. Let's say you have your prescription drug Part D plan, or you happen to have a Medicare Advantage plan. Then what will you do? Well first off, when you're going to move, when you know you're going to move you need to notify your prescription drug plan or your Medicare Advantage plan company. Typically do so 30-days before the month that you're going to move. So, the month before the month you're going to move. Call them up, tell them you're going to move out of their service area. Our new residence is going to be such and such. They will send you a letter. In that letter they're going to say "you're moving out of our service area so we can no longer cover you." It is called a disenrollment letter. That's your free pass. With the disenrollment letter, once you've moved or even as you're planning to move you can then get a new prescription drug plan or new Medicare Advantage plan for the location that you've moved to. You don't have to wait for a annual enrollment , you don't have to wait for anything. It is a special enrollment period for you. You simply apply to the insurance company for a new plan. They will want to see typically the disenrollment letter. That's what gives you the right to get the new plan. No one can deny you coverage. You will simply have a new plan with the new address for where you are now living. One of the best sources for taking a look at and researching your Medicare rights Not just the rights for when you're moving, but then when you can have a special circumstance that will give you the right to purchase another plan. - Tips for Canadian Snowbirds!: Kind: captions Language: en It's Snowbird season! This time of the year, we hear from lots of Canadians driving down or flying into the US, where they're ultimately heading for sunny skies and warmer weather. Can't say I blame them. Aside from being aware of the ever changing COVID testing and quarantine considerations and requirements, here are some important tips for snowbirds to keep in mind when entering the United States. First, know that when a Snowbird applies for entry, they're typically being admitted to the U.S. as a B-2 visitor. Generally, visitors are allowed to stay for up to six months following any given entry, but a CBP or Customs and Border Protection officer can restrict this. When entering the U.S., CBP may ask questions to gauge your intent to return to Canada after your trip within that six month limitation. CBP will not admit someone who intends to possibly stay here permanently or if that person doesn't have the means to support themselves while in the United States. CBP will consider permanent ties back to Canada, like home ownership, financial assets, family ties and the likes. If the CBP officer feels that you're manipulating the rules of a B-2 visitor, they'll probably deny you entry and there's unfortunately not much you can do about it. Most snowbirds, who most snowbirds who come to the United States have the six month rule default concept when they're here. I want to clarify that this isn't an airtight rule when it comes to immigration. While an authorized period of stay following any given entry will probably be limited to six months. CBP does have the authority to allow someone to come into the United States for a longer period than that in any given calendar year. So, for example, let's say a snowbird enters on November first and is admitted for six months through May first. Let's say that person leaves March first, two months shy of that stay, and then they come back to come in on April first. Well, on that April first trip, CBP could technically admit them for an additional six month period of stay, so long as they're satisfied that that individual still has plans to return back to Canada. So you see, there's no technical violation if they stay for that second period of their six month stay because they're still within the terms of their immigration status. Now that's not to say that other considerations don't come into play when it comes to snowbirds and that six month rule. I know that there are sometimes provincial considerations when it comes to health insurance, and of course, there's other tax obligations to factor as well. Finally, let's chat about snowbirds who are nearing the end of one period of six months stay and who don't want to exit the United States. For whatever reason, they want to extend their trip by another two or three months or even up to another six months. Know that that is possible. We can file a paper based application with an agency called U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services or USCIS and request an extension of status. Without that, Canadian having to go back to the United States. Now, ultimately, that Snowbird still has to have plans to leave and and the government will probably ask questions about that or want to see proof of their intent to leave, but know that it can be done. So those are some helpful tips for Canadian snowbirds. If you have any questions about your stay in the United States, contact me Jennifer Behm, a partner with Berardi Immigration Law, and we'll make sure we answer all of your questions.

  8. @1Ma9iN8tive [3 likes]: Testimony of Williamson B.C. Chang, Professor of Law, April 16, 2015 at a meeting of the University of Hawai‘i Board of Regents, University of Hawai‘i at Hilo.

Honorable Board of Regents:

I have had the honor and pleasure to serve as a Professor of Law at the University of Hawai’i for the last 39 years. I have served the University and the community well. I am also grateful for the opportunity to serve and work in the University.

Let me start by saying this: I know a place, I know a country where there would never be a question whether to build an eighteen-story thirty meter telescope on the summit of Mauna Kea. That country, that nation is “Hawai’i.”

Before 1893, it would have been unthinkable that the Government of the Kingdom of Hawai’i would ever conceive of such a plan. Yes, Kalakaua loved astronomy. All Hawaiians loved the stars. However, they loved Mauna Kea even more. Mauna Kea is “sacred” it is the Sky-Father it is the essence, the beginning of the creation chant of the Hawaiian people. All Hawaiians, all Islands, even Taro are descendants of Mauna Kea.

Mauna_Kea_Telescopes

When I say “Mauna Kea” is sacred, I do not mean to use “sacred” the way most people use that term. I mean “sacred” not in the same sense of worship. I use “sacred” in the sense of “precious” and “so important that nothing else counts”—I apply it to those things and people that we care so much about that we would do anything, even flout and break the law, to preserve their existence.

The child of a parent, especially a young child is “sacred” in this sense. So are parents to their children. So are grandparents. Even the family pet is “sacred.” If your house was burning down would you risk your life to go into the burning house to rescue your children, your mother, your grandparents, even your beloved dog or cat? Would you go even if forbidden by first responders, firemen or policemen? Yes, many of us would go without hesitation–without thinking of the consequences. Would you give a kidney to save or extend the life of your child, your brother, your uncle? Would you spend all of your money to save a loved one from cancer? from Lou Gehrig’s disease or from a life in prison without parole? Yes, we all would.

Moreover, we praise such emotions and desires of others who make such sacrifices every day. We understand the soldier who sacrifices himself by instinctively jumping on a grenade. We understand the parent or grandparent who gives all their money to see their child or grandchild through college.

Whether one worships Mauna Kea or not, whether one considers it “sacred” does not matter as much as understanding the instincts that drive those to defend and save Mauna Kea—much as one would understand the absolute love for a child, or a parent even if such acts break the law.

When we see the instinct of family, of brotherhood, of sisterhood of love for mankind in others we celebrate that—we gravitate to that. We love and defend Mauna Kea because it reminds us what makes us human. Sacred is not necessarily a place. It is a relationship, a deep visceral relationship: beyond reason, beyond law, beyond rationality.

The Mauna Kea movement is a movement that has grown because of young people. They live in new confusing world themselves—a world of cognitive dissonance. That is they live within an outright contraction—a Hawaii in decline where there is nothing they can do. They see their world being attacked and destroyed, its water taken, its plants doused with foreign chemicals, its agricultural lands disappear in the name of gentlemen farmers, its open lands used for artillery practice, and its shoreline becoming high-end condominiums that only rich foreigners can afford.

Mauna Kea Protectors 1

Moreover, to the young, Hawaii is unlivable, there is no viable future: There are no places to rent, no jobs that fit their training, no money for retirement and the endless, life-sapping traffic congestion. And now an eighteen story telescope on Mauna Kea!

TMT telescope

It would never be built on other sacred sites: not over the Western Wall, the Dome of the Rock, Angor Wat, Gettysburg, Arlington, or the Arizona Memorial? No one would think of putting a pair of glasses on the eyes of God. Why then, Mauna Kea? We, and our youth are inundated today with the attacks on the treasures of the earth and why?

So, what happened to this “nation” called Hawai‘i, where Mauna Kea was loved and adored? Hawai‘i was a nation, that by a series of events, starting with an overthrow in 1893 and ending with annexation in 1900, by which another nation, the United States, forcefully took the sovereignty of Hawai‘i.

What do I mean by that?—to take one nation’s sovereignty? Sovereignty is the monopoly of a government on the legitimate use of violence.

By that I mean the State, the police and DLNR are the only ones today who can do so-called “legal” violence to Mauna Kea. Similarly, the police of Hawai‘i County and the officers DLNR are the only ones who can use the violence of arrest and jail or fine to force down the protectors of Mauna Kea. Protect the mountain and you go to jail. It is legal. It is called law. It is a power possessed only by the sovereign of a nation. There once was a time in Hawaii when that monopoly on the use of legal power protected not defiled Mauna Kea.

In 1893 and 1900 a new Nation took over in Hawai‘i—a new nation with new rules. These were new rules that had the power to interfere with our very human, emotions and instincts, instincts derived over time from our kupuna, our ancestors and the culture of this nation of Hawai‘i. Hawai‘i has changed.

Today, government has the legitimate power to do violence to families as well. Government agencies can take a child away from a parent. Government agencies can put a Hawaiian in prison for the smallest of offenses—denying him or her freedom and the chance to be with and raise their families. The world of Hawai‘i has been turned upside down.

The answer lies in power, that is law—the shift over their lives by which all is reversed.

In 1898 the United States, by Joint Resolution took the nation of Hawai‘i. I am a legal historian. In the appendix attached I show my work—that concludes definitively that the joint resolution had no such power. It was impotent, it was an act of Congress not a treaty. It could no more take Hawai‘i by a law then Hawai‘i by a law could take America.

It was a fraud—it created a disease that spread, a malaise we all suffer—called the myth of annexation. We all believe we are part of America, we all act as if that were true. We have been taught that way. We follow the lead of others who act that way.

The truth is that the joint resolution did not give to the United States the monopoly on the use of legitimate violence—a violence to build on Mauna Kea, the violence to arrest those who seek to stop that building. Most of all the University claims Mauna Kea by lease—a lease derived from the Joint Resolution.

It is said that the Joint Resolution gave Mauna Kea to the United States, which gave it to the State, which gave it to the University. As a matter of law that is false. It is a lie. The University has no power over Mauna Kea. It cannot build, it cannot give permits, it cannot arrest us.

The mass of young people are here today in protest because we live in a world of cognitive dissonance. They live in a world where they are learning, at the University about the truth of the Joint Resolution, which gives no power, no sovereignty to the state. Outside of their classes they see the State taking what they love—preventing them from running into the burning house to save their Mauna Kea, their father, their sky-father.

And this dissonance makes them ill. It makes our youth sick. It is a crisis that creates mental illness. In short, to build on Mauna Kea is to cast a sickness throughout these islands, a sickness and sadness, not only on Native Hawaiians but on all people who live here.

I have included an appendix, taken from my work, which speaks to the myth of annexation and demonstrates that the Joint Resolution had no capacity to take the Nation of Hawai‘i. I will place this testimony and my appendix on my “Scholar Space” at Hamilton Library, the University of Hawai‘i at Manoa, under my name. This is the link to that site.

Mahalo and Mahalo Ke Akua.
Williamson Chang
Professor of Law

- The 10 Best Countries For Snowbirds: Kind: captions Language: en A snowbird is someone wants to migrate to get&nbsp; away from cold weather and enjoy the warmth,&nbsp;&nbsp; ideally leaving before the first snow&nbsp; and returning home as it begins to thaw.&nbsp; Here are 10 of the best&nbsp; snowbird destinations for 2022.&nbsp; 1. Belize&nbsp; Belize is an affordable piece of quintessential&nbsp; Caribbean that provides a tremendous sense of&nbsp;&nbsp; personal freedom. Here, many people invest in&nbsp; self-sufficient and self-sustainable lifestyles.&nbsp; This country is famous for its white-sand&nbsp; beaches and clear, unpolluted waters.&nbsp; Belize is an English-speaking country that&nbsp; is welcoming and friendly to outsiders. The&nbsp;&nbsp; weather happens to be perfect between December and&nbsp; April, making this an ideal spot for snowbirds.&nbsp; 2. Portugal&nbsp; This small nation is home to breathtaking&nbsp; scenery, varying beautiful landscapes,&nbsp;&nbsp; architectural wonders, and picturesque beaches. Despite its small size,&nbsp;&nbsp; Portugal has a lot to offer visitors. Portugal is affordable, laid-back,&nbsp;&nbsp; and rich in culture, with over 1,794 km of&nbsp; coastline and 300 days a year of sunny skies.&nbsp; No matter what you’re into, there&nbsp; are activities to suit any lifestyle.&nbsp; 3. Costa Rica Rainforests, stunning beaches, great food,&nbsp;&nbsp; and affordable prices… Costa Rica has it all. Costa Rica boasts one of the best climates in&nbsp;&nbsp; the world, having both tropical&nbsp; and subtropical climates.&nbsp; While it doesn’t have the most developed&nbsp; infrastructure, it is a beautiful country&nbsp;&nbsp; where you can discover stunning&nbsp; scenery and wildlife at every turn.&nbsp; Costa Rica is a laid-back getaway perfect&nbsp; for a snowbird to set up their winter nest.&nbsp; 4. Panama&nbsp; Panama, which has been dubbed “the Hub&nbsp; of the Americas,” can arguably be called&nbsp;&nbsp; the best offshore haven in the world. This country is blessed with beautiful&nbsp;&nbsp; rainforests, stunning beaches, soaring&nbsp; mountains, charming islands, and colonial towns.&nbsp; Although Panama City can be a little&nbsp; more expensive, outside the capital,&nbsp;&nbsp; Panama is still a very affordable place to live. It has top-notch healthcare facilities, a friendly&nbsp;&nbsp; population, and a laid-back, easygoing atmosphere. 5. Dominican Republic&nbsp; The Dominican Republic is a beautiful white-sand&nbsp; haven surrounded by warm turquoise waters that&nbsp;&nbsp; enjoys year-round sunshine. This island is affordable and&nbsp;&nbsp; boasts a diverse landscape, including&nbsp; beaches, jungle, and mountain hideaways.&nbsp; The people here are friendly and&nbsp; welcoming, and it is one of the easiest&nbsp;&nbsp; places in the world to establish residency. You’ll always find adventure and excitement,&nbsp;&nbsp; whether it’s in a mountain retreat&nbsp; or one of the charming beach towns.&nbsp; 6. Colombia&nbsp; Colombia is home to some of the best healthcare&nbsp; in Latin America, yet is still a place where you&nbsp;&nbsp; can live a premium lifestyle at a low cost. It is an excellent haven for investors,&nbsp;&nbsp; retirees, and second-home buyers to bypass&nbsp; the typical snowbird escapes like Florida or&nbsp;&nbsp; Arizona in exchange for adventure, romance, and&nbsp; a high-class lifestyle for an affordable price.&nbsp; On top of all that, Colombia has stunning&nbsp; white-sand beaches that lead to crystal clear&nbsp;&nbsp; blue waters, great weather, colonial cities,&nbsp; a vibrant culture, and loads of activities to&nbsp;&nbsp; keep visitors busy. 7. Malta&nbsp; Malta has a rugged coastline&nbsp; with stunning cliffs, beaches,&nbsp;&nbsp; quaint fishing harbors, coves,&nbsp; and ancient forts to explore.&nbsp; The laid-back vibe of this island makes&nbsp; it an excellent destination for retirees,&nbsp;&nbsp; and people who love the sun and outdoors. On top of that, Malta offers top-notch healthcare,&nbsp;&nbsp; the island is very safe, the quality of&nbsp; life is high, and the cost of living is low.&nbsp; 8. Malaysia Malaysia is&nbsp;&nbsp; an excellent place to spend your winters if you&nbsp; are interested in learning a lot about different&nbsp;&nbsp; cultures without spending the whole winter&nbsp; bouncing around from one place to another.&nbsp; This country is exotic, with stunning beaches&nbsp; and a low cost of living, yet it is safe,&nbsp;&nbsp; and has excellent healthcare… to the point where&nbsp; the country is now a medical tourism hot spot.&nbsp; 9. Vietnam&nbsp; Vietnam is a peaceful country that could&nbsp; arguably be called the cheapest place to&nbsp;&nbsp; live on Earth, depending on your lifestyle. It is a very tourist- and expat-friendly country,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the government makes an effort to treat&nbsp; foreigners well to attract more visitors.&nbsp; Vietnam is a land of breathtaking, world-class&nbsp; beaches, beautiful mountain retreats,&nbsp;&nbsp; and vibrant towns. 10. Mexico&nbsp; Mexico is easily the most accessible&nbsp; country from the United States and Canada,&nbsp;&nbsp; and one of the most popular expat&nbsp; destinations in the Americas.&nbsp; Mexico is a vast country with endless lifestyle&nbsp; options. Mexico has mountains, rainforests,&nbsp;&nbsp; two long coastlines, colonial cities,&nbsp; Mayan ruins, jungles, rivers, and lakes.&nbsp; The proximity to the United States, the low cost&nbsp; of living, and the sheer variety of lifestyle&nbsp;&nbsp; options make Mexico an easy option for&nbsp; expats seeking a winter-proof haven.&nbsp; Which of these snowbird&nbsp; destinations call your name?&nbsp; Let us know in the comments!

- Canada Snowbirds Abandon Florida | $6.6B Lost, Tourism & Housing Crisis Hits Hard: Kind: captions Language: en For President Trump's latest round of tariffs, air travel between Canada and Florida has dropped off. [Music] >> That's something noticed by Fort Lauderdale tourism officials. >> It's been um concerning. Uh Canadian tourism is about is down about 20%. Um Canada's our number one uh visitor market, so it's um disturbing. We're in a much better position. We have trillions of dollars coming into our country. If we didn't have tariffs, we would be a a very poor nation. >> He's attacking Canadian families, workers, and businesses. Florida, the sunshine state once painted as the ultimate winter paradise for Canadians, is facing a collapse no one thought possible. For decades, it was more than just a warm escape. It was a ritual. Retirees from across Canada, often called snowbirds, would flock south every winter, bringing with them not just vacation spirit, but billions of dollars that fueled local economies. Beach towns thrived, restaurants buzzed, and whole communities depended on their steady return year after year. But today, that golden lifeline has cracked wide open. Shuttered storefronts now line boardwalks where Canadian laughter once echoed. Business owners whisper the same chilling words. Ghost Town. At John's Pass, one of Florida's most iconic waterfront strips. Revenues have already plummeted by 30% this year alone. The numbers are staggering. In just 6 months, Florida has lost an estimated $6.6 billion in Canadian spending. Alongside it vanished $2.1 billion in property taxes once secured by foreign seasonal residents. Town budgets, once comfortably cushioned by steady Canadian cash, are now riddled with shortfalls. Local leaders face impossible choices. Cut community services, raise taxes on struggling residents, or sink deeper into deficit. And the collapse is not only on the ground, it's in the skies above. Flights once brimming with Canadians heading to Miami, Tampa, or Fort Lauderdale are now flying half empty. Airline data shows a 76% drop in ticket sales from Canada to Florida compared with the same month a year earlier. Industry veterans call it unprecedented. Airports once designed to welcome crowds of seasonal visitors now operate at a fraction of their capacity. Terminals stand quiet, echoing with emptiness. Regional airports built and expanded in anticipation of endless Canadian arrivals are slashing routes by nearly 40%. What was once an asset is now a financial burden. For airlines like Air Canada, the silence is deafening. Flights from Toronto that used to sell out months in advance now leave with rows of empty seats. Crews describe it bluntly. We've never seen anything like this before. And behind every empty flight, every closed storefront, lies the same harsh truth. Canadians are no longer just skipping a season. They're walking away altogether. The retreat isn't temporary. It's decisive. In cities like Tampa, St. Petersburg, and Clearwater, demand for second homes has plunged by 25% in the first quarter of 2025. Families who once dreamed of Florida sunshine are now rushing to offload their investments. In Arizona, one real estate agency reports 18 Canadian-owned vacation homes suddenly listed for sale, five to six times higher than usual. Even families who lose thousands in deposits are choosing to cancel their trips. redirecting their winters to Mexico, Cuba, or even southern Europe. For Florida, it's more than lost tourism. It's the unraveling of an economy built on trust, stability, and loyalty. The question is no longer when Canadians will return. The question is, will they ever return at all? The cracks in Florida's winter economy are spreading fast, reaching into industries far beyond tourism. What began as a dip in airline tickets and vacation rentals has grown into a full-blown economic contagion. The Canadian exodus is leaving behind empty neighborhoods, silent clinics, and collapsing businesses. Take healthcare. For decades, Florida's clinics and hospitals thrived on the seasonal arrival of Canadian retirees. Thousands of patients would seek long-term care, routine checkups, and specialized treatments during their winter stay. This steady flow created a dependable income stream, keeping entire medical practices afloat. Now that lifeline is gone. Revenues at seasonal care centers have plunged by more than 60%. Specialists who once relied on predictable Canadian appointments stare at empty calendars. Some have closed their offices entirely, leaving communities without access to crucial services. The construction and home maintenance sector has been battered just as severely. Canadian-owned properties once provided year-round work for contractors. Seasonal renovations, repairs, landscaping, and upgrades kept crews busy and cash flowing. But now, contracts have collapsed by 70%. Contractors scramble for work, and small businesses that once relied on Canadian clients are downsizing or shutting their doors. The housing market itself is cracking under the strain. In neighborhoods designed around seasonal living, property values have fallen by as much as 30%. For sale signs line the streets, but buyers are nowhere to be found. Canadian homeowners are desperate to sell, flooding the market, while American demand is far too weak to absorb the excess. Entire streets that once buzzed with seasonal life now stand eerily silent. Windows shuttered and lawns unkempt. Behind these economic tremors lies politics. Florida, a state that built its identity on hospitality and openness, is now paying the price for choosing ideology over pragmatism. Instead of recalibrating when the first cracks appeared, leaders doubled down. They delivered fiery sound bites and defiant slogans, ignoring the mounting cost to local economies. The result, a credibility crisis that stretches across industries and even across borders. Leaked reports from tourism boards and chambers of commerce reveal the panic behind closed doors. Executives admit the losses are not just seasonal, they are structural. Hotels face waves of cancellations. Budgets once balanced by Canadian property taxes are sinking into deficit. Projections show shortfalls stretching years into the future. And perhaps the most devastating realization, this isn't a cycle that will simply correct itself with the next season sun. This is a permanent redirection of loyalty. Canadians once thought to be bound to Florida by climate are discovering alternatives. And those alternatives are more than willing to welcome them. The exodus is not just about missed vacations. It's about trust. Once trust is broken, it rarely returns. Florida gambled on loyalty, assuming Canadians would always come back for the warmth of its beaches. But economics is unforgiving, and the billions once taken for granted are now flowing abroad, reshaping the map of winter tourism forever. While Florida stumbles, rivals abroad are moving swiftly to seize the opportunity. They're not just waiting for Canadian travelers to show up. They are actively reshaping their policies, infrastructure, and messaging to capture the loyalty Florida squandered. Mexico, already a favorite destination for Canadians, is doubling down. Beyond the natural advantages of affordability and warm beaches, it's simplifying visa processes, extending residency options, and promoting itself as more than just a vacation spot. In coastal hubs like Puerto Valarta and Merida, developers are building new communities designed specifically to mirror the suburban comfort Canadians once sought in Florida. These aren't just condos. They're carefully crafted neighborhoods offering familiarity, safety, and long-term stability. Across the Atlantic, Portugal is taking a more strategic approach. Its government has launched campaigns not just about sunshine, but about trust, community, and security. Tax incentives for retirees, targeted marketing in Canadian cities, and a promise of political stability are all part of the pitch. The message is clear. You will belong here, and you won't have to worry about political storms derailing your plans. Then there's the Caribbean, a region that has always attracted winter travelers, but now stands ready to permanently replace Florida's role. Barbados, the Dominican Republic, and the Bahamas are repositioning themselves as more than resorts. They're branding as havens of hospitality where Canadians are not just tolerated for their money, but embraced as valued guests. Airlines are partnering with resorts to create seamless packages, making it easier than ever for Canadians to skip Florida entirely. Each of these destinations is exploiting a truth Florida ignored. Loyalty is fragile. Once lost, it migrates quickly. The billions in Canadian disposable income that once poured into Florida's economy are now fueling growth in Mexico, Portugal, and across the Caribbean. And unlike Florida, these regions are building long-term strategies to ensure that loyalty sticks. Meanwhile, the domino effect back home is accelerating. Entire industries tethered to seasonal Canadian demand are collapsing in tandem. Car rental companies, airport shuttles, catering services. Fleets of vehicles now sit idle in parking lots, gathering dust. Mechanics and drivers are laid off in waves. Restaurants that once thrived on winter crowds are closing sections, reducing staff or shutting down altogether. Even the health care sector continues to unravel. Pharmacies and hospitals that once relied on Canadian prescriptions and checkups are cutting staff or closing departments. administrators face impossible decisions, merging facilities or pulling out of communities entirely. The absence of Canadians is leaving behind not just economic gaps, but service deserts that local residents now struggle to navigate. And the blow to real estate is perhaps the most visible of all. Property tax revenues that funded schools, libraries, and community programs have evaporated. Local governments face painful budget cuts. Some neighborhoods once lush with well-kept Canadian-owned homes now show signs of decay, unckempt lawns, shuttered windows, entire streets sinking into eerie silence. What began as a tourism hiccup has mutated into a structural break. Florida is learning the hard way that when a single source of demand disappears, the collapse spreads in ways no one can fully contain. The deeper lesson of Florida's unraveling lies not just in the numbers, but in the signal it sends to the world. If even Canada, America's closest ally, its neighbor, its most dependable partner, can walk away this quickly, who might be next? The figures are brutal in their clarity. A single Canadian household spends on average $33,000 over 6 months in Florida. To replace that one household, it would take 28 American households. That's not just lost tourism. It's the collapse of a core customer base that was both predictable and loyal. And loyalty, once gone, does not come back easily. Florida built its winter economy on an assumption that climate alone would keep Canadians tethered to its beaches. That no matter the politics, snowbirds would always return. But the boycott proved otherwise. Economics is more powerful than sunshine. And when billions in revenue migrate elsewhere, they take with them tax dollars, jobs, and community investments that Florida once took for granted. The consequences are staggering. Schools that once benefited from seasonal property tax revenue are now cutting programs. Libraries and community centers close early, or not at all. Local governments are forced to slash services, leaving residents to bear the brunt of budget shortfalls they didn't cause. Neighborhoods that once glowed with seasonal life are fading into emptiness, their decline visible to anyone who dares to drive through. What began as a tourism shock has mutated into a full-scale economic contagion. One that is cascading across sectors, destabilizing entire towns and shaking investor confidence. And the collapse is not just Florida's problem. It's a warning to the entire United States. Once political hostility fractures economic trust, the damage spreads far beyond state lines. Investors, businesses, and policymakers are now confronted with a chilling truth. When ideology becomes the stage, the economy becomes the casualty. Florida elevated political defiance over pragmatic cooperation and in doing so undermine the very foundation of its prosperity. The cost is measured not only in billions of dollars, but in credibility. And credibility is the hardest currency to regain. Meanwhile, competitors abroad are thriving. Mexico's snowbird communities expand. Portugal wins retirees with promises of security, and Caribbean resorts welcome a surge of Canadian visitors. The disposable income Florida once relied on is now cementing loyalty elsewhere. And with every passing year, the chance of winning that loyalty back grows smaller. So the question remains, if America cannot hold on to the trust of its closest neighbor, how will it fair in the eyes of the rest of the world? What if a prolonged war in the Middle East actually ended up supporting Florida real estate demand? Not because war is good for the economy. It's not. But because when global uncertainty rises, capital behaves in very predictable ways. Money looks for safety, currencies move, energy prices spike, and investors start asking a simple question. Where in the world is my money safest? Today, we're going to walk through that chain reaction step by step. First, we'll look at the global economy ripple effects if the Iran war persists. Then, in part two, we'll look at a counterintuitive scenario, why that same conflict could actually increase demand for Florida real estate, especially among Canadian investors. Part one. If the Iran war persists, likely economic ripple effects. Iran's current strategy, according to many analysts, is not necessarily to defeat the United States militarily. It's something different. The strategy is to raise the economic cost of the war. And the way to do that is by targeting the global economy's pressure points. The biggest one is energy. Energy shock. About 20% of the world's oil and LNG natural gas shipments normally pass through the straight of Hormuz. If that shipping lane becomes disrupted, even partially, global oil prices respond almost immediately. And oil is not just about gasoline. Oil effects, transportation, agriculture, plastics, airlines, logistics, manufacturing, construction materials. In other words, oil is embedded in almost everything we buy. So when energy prices spike, inflation tends to follow. Shipping and trade disruptions. Energy is only part of the equation. Shipping routes through the Persian Gulf become riskier during conflict. When that happens, shipping insurance costs rise, cargo routes are rerouted, delivery times lengthen, freight costs increase. The result is supply chain friction, and that friction raises the price of physical goods around the world. inflation returns. Energy shocks historically reignite inflation. And when inflation rises, central banks usually respond cautiously. Instead of cutting interest rates, they tend to keep rates higher for longer. That affects mortgage rates, business loans, construction financing, investment activity. In other words, a prolonged conflict could keep global financial conditions tight. Currency effects. During geopolitical crises, investors tend to move toward what are considered safe haven assets. This is called the flight to safety. The biggest safe haven asset in the world is still the US dollar. So during periods of uncertainty, global capital flows into US Treasury bonds. Investors hold more US dollars. The dollar often strengthens relative to other currencies. That has consequences for other economies. How about Canada's economy? Canada sits in an interesting position during energy shocks. Canada is a major oil exporter, which means higher oil prices can benefit the country's trade balance. Energy provinces like Alberta often see stronger revenues and investment, but Canadian consumers still feel the pain of higher fuel costs. So, Canada can experience a strange combination of stronger energy exports, higher domestic inflation, slower consumer spending. Meanwhile, if the US dollar strengthens globally, the Canadian dollar often weakens slightly in comparison. That matters for Canadian investors looking south. How about the US economy? While the US produces a lot of energy, it's still highly sensitive to oil prices. Higher oil prices raise transportation costs, airline tickets, manufacturing inputs, food prices. When households spend more on energy, they tend to spend less on discretionary purchases. That creates a mild stagflation risk. Slower growth combined with persistent The Federal Reserve may then delay interest rate cuts and that keeps borrowing costs elevated. US real estate higher borrowing costs affect housing markets. The typical pattern is not an immediate price collapse. Instead, what tends to happen is transaction volumes fall, buyers hesitate, sales cycles lengthen. Housing supply in many US markets is still limited, which tends to slow price declines, but higher rates reduce affordability. How about Florida real estate? Florida has become quite unique in economic dynamics. The state continues to benefit from population migration, favorable tax policies, tourism, and service industries. However, inflation raises the cost of construction materials, insurance, and labor. That affects new development, renovation costs, operating expenses for property owners. How about Orlando real estate? Orlando's housing market is influenced heavily by population growth, tourism employment, service sector expansion, new multif family construction. If interest rates remain elevated, home sales slow, rent growth moderates, occupancy remains relatively stable, rental demand often stays strong because higher mortgage rates keep some households renting longer. Now, what about local service businesses? Businesses that rely on discretionary spending, like home remodeling, tend to feel the pressure first. Consumers become more cautious. Typical changes include longer decision times, increased price sensitivity, smaller project scopes. But renovation demand rarely disappears completely. Homeowners still renovate when kitchens fail, appliances break, or properties need updating. Successful businesses during inflationary periods tend to focus on value, durability, and predictable pricing. Part two, the counterintuitive scenario for Orlando. Now, here's where things get interesting, because the global chain reaction we just discussed can produce a surprising side effect. A prolonged Middle East conflict could actually increase demand for Florida real estate. That sounds strange at first, but the mechanism is fairly straightforward. The global safety trade. During geopolitical crisis, investors often look for places where their wealth feels safer. That usually means three things: stable currencies, strong property rights, liquid real estate markets. The United States checks all three boxes and Florida happens to be one of the easiest entry points for international buyers. The currency hedge. If the US dollar strengthens during global uncertainty, international investors sometimes move capital into US dollar denominated assets. Real estate is one of the most common choices. Why? Because property can act as both an investment and a lifestyle asset. Florida offers something few markets can combine. Warm climate, relatively favorable taxes, international accessibility, strong tourism demand. What's Canada's role in all this? For Canadian investors, currency movements can make this dynamic even more interesting. If the US dollar strengthens relative to the Canadian dollar, some investors become cautious. But wealthier investors often react differently. They view US assets as a currency diversification strategy. Owning property in Florida effectively pays places part of their wealth inside the US economic system. That can feel like a form of financial insurance. What this means for Canadian snowbird investors. So, what does all of this mean if you're a Canadian investor looking at Florida? First, understand that global conflicts affect markets through indirect economic channels, energy prices, interest rates, currency movements, capital flows. Those forces can tighten financial conditions globally, but they can also reinforce the attractiveness of stable jurisdictions with strong property rights. Florida has historically been one of those destinations, especially for Canadians seeking lifestyle diversification, currency diversification, long-term real estate exposure. A prolonged geopolitical conflict does not automatically mean Florida real estate weakens. In some scenarios, it may even reinforce international demand. Because when uncertainty rises, investors around the world start asking the same question. Where should part of my life and my capital live? A second home isn't just a real estate transaction. It's a fivemonth economic engine. Florida isn't losing tourists. It's losing residents, the kind who stay half the year, spend like locals, and keep entire neighborhoods alive. And once they leave, they don't come back. Florida officials insist this is temporary, a minor fluctuation. But the numbers tell a different story. This isn't a pause. It's an exodus and it's accelerating. That's not a dip. That's structural failure. Airlines are already cutting flights. WestJet and Air Canada have reduced seasonal routes into Florida, reallocating capacity to Mexico, Jamaica, and Portugal, where traveler demand is increasing. Recently, he's seeing about one solid reservation per day, a shadow of what he used to book every hour. He's cautiously optimistic, but even he admits this isn't seasonal slowdown. This is a shift. The Atlantic Hotel and Spa joined these outreach missions, openly acknowledging that Canadian traffic plunged so severely they could no longer rely on their core market. It's about economic structure. Florida built entire ecosystems around long-term seasonal residents and is now trying to replace them with short-term tourists who can't possibly replicate that impact. When Canadians sell their condos, stop buying appliances, cancel insurance policies, skip doctor visits, and stop tipping $20 on breakfast because that's just what they do. The economy notices, but not immediately, slowly, silently, then suddenly. Even public infrastructure feels it. National Travel Data shows Canadians backing away from the United States altogether. Statistics Canada reported that in July 2025, Canadians returning by car from the US dropped 36.9% from 2024 levels. Air returns were down 25.8%. And August continued the trend. It's about a growing emotional and political divide between two countries that used to treat each other like friendly siblings. Think about this. In 2024, 20.2 million Canadians visited the United States. They spent over 20 billion supporting 140,000 American jobs. Now those numbers are collapsing nationwide. Even Representative Jared Moscowitz, a Florida lawmaker, admitted Canadian tourism in parts of Broward and Palm Beach counties was down about 80%. Fact checkers tried to calm the number, saying it wasn't officially that high, but even their conservative estimate of a 20% drop is enough to send local businesses into panic. The Canadian retreat is unlike anything Florida has faced. Hurricanes come and go. Red tide hits one year and clears the next. Recessions slow spending but recover. But when a loyal traveler feels unwelcome, they don't pause, they choose elsewhere. So where are they going? Mexico is the new Florida for thousands of former snowbirds. All inclusive resorts offer multimonth packages at prices cheaper than a Florida condo's insurance bill. Medical care is affordable. Locals are friendly. No one is fingerprinting them at the border or asking how long they plan to stay. The Caribbean, especially the Dominican Republic, Barbados and Turks and Kaikos, offers familiar beaches without the political static. Portugal and Spain are seeing a surge in European style snowbirds who want history, mild winters, healthcare access, and lower rent. And here's the most crucial line. Most Canadians leaving Florida aren't taking a break. They're closing a chapter. They're selling their Florida homes. They're building new social lives elsewhere. They're watching Spanish sunsets instead of clear water ones. They're joining Facebook groups like Canadian snowbirds in Portugal instead of Canadians in Naples, Florida. The next 6 months will decide whether Florida corrects course or locks in a long-term economic decline disguised beneath stable headline numbers. State officials keep pointing to overall tourism figures, proudly noting that Florida welcomed 75.3 million visitors in the first half of 2025, only 0 1% lower than the same period in 2024. On paper, that seems fine. To the untrained eye, nothing is wrong. But look deeper. Domestic tourism, especially day trippers and short-term vacationers from other US states, has stayed strong. Meanwhile, international tourism is falling sharply. And Canada, historically Florida's most valuable foreign market, is leading that decline. That trade-off is dangerous. You can't replace a 75year-old snowbird who rents a condo for 5 months, tips generously at breakfast every morning, buys furniture, pays HOA dues, donates to local churches, attends community fish fries with a family of four who spends 3 days at Disney, eats off property once, and flies home. But that's how the data is being interpreted. State leaders see big visitor numbers and assume everything's fine. Small business owners see empty bar stools and cancelled bookings and know everything is not fine. The most overlooked issue, generational transfer. Many of the Canadians currently selling their Florida homes are in their 70s and 80s. People who have been coming for 20, 30, sometimes 40 winters. They didn't just love Florida. They were part of it. They knew their neighbors. They had traditions. They went to the same restaurants every week. They were regulars at church bingo nights. Now they're aging out of travel. And instead of passing their Florida lifestyle to their children, they're closing that book entirely. Younger Canadians don't want the same kind of snowbird life. They're not buying second homes. They're not interested in spending five straight months in one place. They want flexibility, multiple shorter trips across different countries. They'd rather backpack Portugal and Costa Rica than sit on one beach until April. And Florida is doing absolutely nothing to appeal to them. No new incentives, no visa flexibility for longer stays, no tax accommodations, no insurance reform, no warm messaging. Instead, they hear, "Come if you want, but you'll pay more and don't expect gratitude." Compare that to Portugal, which now offers longstay visas specifically for retirees and remote workers. Spain advertises aggressively to Canadians, promising lower cost of living, access to public health care, and tax friendly residency options. Mexico has streamlined customs for extended visitors and welcomes Canadian snowbirds with no fingerprinting, no suspicion, and no lectures. In other words, Florida isn't being outpriced. It's being outhospitable. Some tourism experts say Florida could turn this around, but only if leadership acknowledges the crisis instead of denying it. Here's what could help. public messaging that explicitly welcomes Canadian visitors without sarcasm or condescension. Insurance reforms or incentive programs that help foreign property owners keep their homes. Bilateral talks with Canada reassuring long-term visitors that they're still wanted. Travel marketing campaigns built around nostalgia reminding older snowbirds of the Florida they fell in love with before it slips away. But none of that happens unless state leaders stop pretending everything's fine. And that's the real crisis. Florida won't wake up one morning to find all its Canadian snowbirds gone. Instead, it'll wake up and realize they've been leaving quietly, steadily, for years. And by the time the state notices, they'll already be happily settled in Mexico, Portugal, or the Caribbean. By then, the condos will be sold. The airport routes will be cancelled. The local diners will be shuttered on weekday mornings. And the economy will adapt, but painfully. Residents who once benefited from snowbird tax revenue may suddenly face higher local taxes. Service sector workers may lose hours or entire jobs. Real estate markets could flatten for years. The danger isn't immediate collapse. its slow decay disguised as stability. Right now in October 2025, Florida stands in a moment of denial versus reality. On one side are politicians saying everything's great. On the other are cafe owners, realtors, hotel managers, and retirees who feel the shift every single day. the empty rental properties, the quiet beaches in February, the for sale signs on once coveted condos, the Facebook groups full of Canadians saying, "Where are you going now that you're done with Florida?" Those are the real metrics. This isn't about tourism. This is about relationships that took decades to build and are now dissolving in silence. Canada didn't officially turn its back on Florida. The next few months will determine whether Florida realizes just how much it depended on Canadian snowbirds and whether those snowbirds can be persuaded to return. Comment below if you've noticed changes where you live. Are the Canadian plates disappearing from your streets? Are the breakfast spots quieter this winter? And if you're watching from Canada, be honest. Are you still booking Florida this season? or have you moved on to Mexico, Portugal, or somewhere cheaper and friendlier? Because Florida is about to learn the hard way what happens when loyalty is taken for granted. But there's one part of this story I haven't revealed yet. Why did Florida make it so difficult for the very people who kept its economy afloat for decades? How did a state built on hospitality turn confrontational and restrictive overnight? The truth is deeper and more calculated than you think. In fact, I uncover exactly how that shift happened in the video appearing on your screen right now. Watch it to see how policy decisions, not hurricanes or inflation, triggered this collapse. Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next
